ACMR.relative_comps: ACM Research is a China-focused wafer-cleaning equipment maker whose true peers are LRCX, AMAT, and KLAC in etch/clean, plus smaller-cap ONTO and COHU in process control. ACMR trades at 0 reported p_fcf (FCF negative or suppressed), a negative graham MoS (-46%), and modest net margins of 10%, far below LRCX/KLAC which carry 25-30% net margins with 60-80x p_fcf premiums. Revenue growth at 15% YoY and 32% 3Y CAGR is strong but partially driven by China tariff-driven capacity pull-forward, creating concentration risk that the valuation does not fully price in. Proximity to 52-week high of 92% suggests the market has already re-rated the China-semiconductor tailwind; relative to true peers with broader geographic diversification, ACMR looks in-line to slightly rich once geopolitical discount is properly applied.
ACMR.rationale: Wafer-cleaning/surface prep equipment maker, primarily serving China fabs. Direct competitors are the clean/etch divisions of LRCX, AMAT, and at smaller scale ONTO and COHU. The ticker is unambiguously ACM Research (NASDAQ: ACMR), confirmed by Technology/Semiconductors classification and China-focused revenue profile.

ADBE.relative_comps: Adobe is the dominant creative-software and digital-experience platform, with true peers in enterprise SaaS (CRM, NOW, WDAY) and productivity suites (MSFT). ADBE's p_fcf of ~8.8x and multi-method MoS spread shows genuine cheapness: DCF MoS +45%, graham MoS +69%, owner earnings +38%, iv15 +41% — a rare cross-methodology convergence for a high-quality compounder. Gross margins of 89% and net margins of 30% with ROIC of 27% are best-in-class among peers; CRM and NOW trade at 30-60x FCF with lower margins, making ADBE cheap vs peers on every metric. The 52-week position near the low (proximity 6%) and SMA200 discount reflect AI-disruption fears that appear overstated given Adobe's Firefly monetization and workflow moat.
ADBE.rationale: Adobe Inc., the creative and digital-experience software platform (Photoshop, Acrobat, Experience Cloud). Peers are enterprise SaaS platforms and creative-productivity software vendors. Multiple valuation methods converge on undervaluation vs. the peer group.

AMD.relative_comps: AMD competes directly with NVDA in datacenter GPU/AI accelerators and with INTC in x86 CPUs; AVGO and MRVL are adjacent in custom silicon. AMD's p_fcf of ~127x and all valuation MoS scores negative or absent confirm it is priced for continued execution on AI datacenter wins. Revenue growth of 34% YoY and gross margins of 50% lag NVDA's 75%+ gross margins and near-term AI dominance, making AMD a weaker-momentum second place at a similar valuation premium. Proximity to 52-week high of 90% and strong SMA200 momentum confirm the market agrees with the bullish trajectory. Relative to NVDA, AMD is cheaper on absolute multiples but the quality gap (margins, moat) justifies a discount; relative to INTC it is richer but rightfully so given execution superiority.
AMD.rationale: Advanced Micro Devices, x86 CPU and GPU designer competing directly with NVDA (AI/datacenter GPU) and INTC (CPU). The sector/industry tag Semiconductors and the data metrics (34% revenue growth, 50% gross margin) confirm the identity unambiguously.

AMS.MC.relative_comps: AMS.MC is Amadeus IT Group (Madrid), the global travel-technology and GDS platform — NOT a semiconductor company despite the ticker similarity. Peers are travel-tech/IT-services companies: Amadeus competes with Sabre (SABR), Travelport (private), and broadly with large IT-services firms (ACN, CAP.PA, CTSH) for enterprise tech outsourcing. p_fcf of ~10x and positive MoS across DCF (+12%), graham (+67%), and iv15 (+31%) confirm moderate cheapness; gross margins of 28% and net margins of 20% are consistent with a software-heavy distribution platform. Revenue growth of 6% YoY and 13% 3Y CAGR reflects post-COVID GDS recovery, and the 52-week proximity of 22% signals the market has partially re-rated but not fully captured the post-pandemic re-engagement. Vs. ACN or CTSH (IT services), Amadeus carries higher margins and a stronger toll-road moat, suggesting it is cheap relative to quality.
AMS.MC.rationale: Amadeus IT Group S.A. (BME: AMS), the global travel-technology / GDS (global distribution system) platform for airlines, hotels, and travel agencies. The .MC suffix confirms Madrid listing. NOT a semiconductor. Peers are travel-tech and enterprise IT-services platforms.

ANET.relative_comps: Arista Networks is the hyperscale and cloud datacenter networking leader, with CSCO as primary incumbent competitor and JNPR/EXTR in enterprise switching. ANET's p_fcf of ~47x is well above CSCO's ~15x but the quality premium is largely justified: gross margins of 64% vs CSCO ~63%, but ANET's net margin of 39% and ROIC of 22% handily beat CSCO (~25% net, ~15% ROIC). Revenue growth of 29% YoY and 27% 3Y CAGR vs CSCO's low-single-digits makes ANET a genuine growth-adjusted value relative to the networking peer group. The 52-week proximity of 73% and SMA200 premium confirm momentum alignment. Relative to pure-growth networking plays like INFN or CIEN, ANET trades richer but with far superior margin structure; vs CSCO on a growth-adjusted basis, ANET looks in-line.
ANET.rationale: Arista Networks, cloud and enterprise datacenter ethernet switching hardware/software. Computer Hardware sector classification in the data confirms identity. Direct peers are network infrastructure vendors CSCO, JNPR, EXTR, HPE and merchant-silicon adjacent NVDA (BlueField).

ARM.relative_comps: Arm Holdings is the dominant CPU IP licensor (AArch64 ISA) with no listed direct competitor — x86 INTC and AMD are architectural rivals, while QCOM, AVGO, and MRVL are major licensees/customers. ARM's p_fcf of ~365x and all MoS metrics maxed negative reflect extreme premium pricing for a near-monopoly on CPU ISA royalties. Gross margins of 92% and net margins of 18% are consistent with a software/IP royalty business, though net margins appear compressed by heavy R&D reinvestment. Revenue growth of 23% YoY reflects AI-driven silicon proliferation across smartphones, servers, and IoT. At 365x FCF ARM trades materially richer than any hardware semiconductor peer — the justification rests entirely on secular share gains and royalty rate escalation in AI chips, which is plausible but already priced in.
ARM.rationale: Arm Holdings plc (NASDAQ: ARM), the CPU IP architecture licensor whose AArch64 ISA underpins virtually all mobile and increasingly server chips. 92% gross margin and Semiconductors classification confirm identity. Priced as a toll-road monopoly; closest structural analogs are software platforms, not traditional semiconductor companies.

ASML.relative_comps: ASML is the global monopoly supplier of EUV lithography equipment; no public company offers a competitive EUV product, making AMAT, LRCX, and KLA the closest listed peers by industry segment (semiconductor capital equipment). ASML's p_fcf of ~61x and negative MoS across most methods confirm a premium valuation that reflects the monopoly moat; gross margins of 53% and net margins of 29% with ROIC of 31% are best-in-class in capex equipment. Revenue growth of 16% YoY and 16% 3Y CAGR is steady but below the AI-accelerated capex spend, and the 52-week proximity of 87% and SMA200 premium confirm strong momentum. Relative to AMAT (~28x FCF, 48% gross margin) and LRCX (~35x FCF, 47% gross margin), ASML's monopoly premium is partially justified but leaves limited further upside at current levels.
ASML.rationale: ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ: ASML), the sole supplier of EUV photolithography systems for leading-edge chip manufacturing. Semiconductors industry tag and 53% gross margin confirm identity. The monopoly position justifies a significant premium, but the multiple is stretched relative to the broader equipment peer group.

ATE.PA.relative_comps: ATE.PA is Alten S.A. (Euronext Paris), a French engineering and technology consulting/staffing firm specializing in R&D outsourcing for aerospace, auto, and defense. True peers are IT and engineering services firms: Capgemini (CAP.PA), Cognizant (CTSH), and European engineering consultancies like Akka Technologies (acquired) and Altran (merged into Capgemini). Alten's p_fcf of ~5.5x is extremely cheap vs CAP.PA at ~6.7x and CTSH at ~7.4x, with positive multi-method MoS (DCF +62%, owner earnings +52%, EPV +44%) — a genuine value case. Gross margins of 8% reflect a staffing/consulting pass-through model (consistent with the sector), net margins 2.6% are thin but ROIC of 11% is acceptable. The 52-week proximity of 8% and below-SMA200 price confirm the market has de-rated it; relative to peers on FCF yield, ATE.PA looks cheap.
ATE.PA.rationale: Alten S.A. (EPA: ATE), French engineering and IT consulting firm providing R&D outsourcing to aerospace, automotive, and telecom clients. The .PA suffix confirms Paris listing; Information Technology Services classification, 8% gross margin, and low p_fcf confirm the staffing/consulting model. NOT a semiconductor or materials tester.

AVGO.relative_comps: Broadcom is a diversified semiconductor and infrastructure software company (after the VMware acquisition), competing with QCOM in networking/wireless chips, MRVL in custom silicon, TXN in analog, and now MSFT/VMware in enterprise software. At p_fcf of ~66x, all MoS metrics are negative, reflecting a premium paid for the compounding acquisition machine and AI custom chip franchise. Gross margins of 68% and net margins of 36% are outstanding for a diversified semi — above QCOM (~58% gross, ~25% net) but below NVDA's AI-inflected margins. Revenue growth of 24% YoY driven by VMware consolidation and AI ASIC wins; 52-week proximity of 44% suggests some correction from peak. Relative to QCOM (~20x FCF) and TXN (~30x FCF), AVGO is richer, but the software-heavy recurring revenue from VMware partially warrants that premium.
AVGO.rationale: Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ: AVGO), diversified fabless semiconductor and infrastructure software (VMware, CA Technologies). Semiconductors classification and 68% gross margin confirm identity. Competes in networking ASICs, storage controllers, wireless, and now enterprise software.

CAP.PA.relative_comps: Capgemini is Europe's largest IT services and consulting group, competing directly with CTSH, Infosys (INFY), Wipro (WIT), and Accenture (ACN). p_fcf of ~6.7x and owner earnings MoS of +52% and DCF MoS of +45% make CAP.PA modestly cheap, consistent with a mature IT-services business trading below intrinsic value. Gross margins of 25% and net margins of 7% are typical for the labor-intensive IT services model; ROIC of 9% is adequate. Revenue growth of just 2% YoY and 1% 3Y CAGR reflects the post-pandemic normalization of IT spend; the 52-week proximity of 5% and below-SMA200 position signal continued de-rating. Relative to CTSH (p_fcf 7.4x, better growth momentum) and ATE.PA (p_fcf 5.5x), CAP.PA sits in the middle — in-line overall, with limited catalyst for re-rating absent a significant AI-services revenue inflection.
CAP.PA.rationale: Capgemini SE (EPA: CAP), French multinational IT services and consulting company. The .PA suffix and Information Technology Services classification with 25% gross margins confirm the identity as a large-cap European IT outsourcer.

CRDO.relative_comps: Credo Technology is a high-speed connectivity semiconductor company (SerDes, retimers, active electrical cables) serving hyperscale AI/datacenter builds. True peers are MRVL (closest: high-speed datacenter interconnect), ANET (networking), and optical connectivity players COHR and AAOI. At p_fcf of ~110x and all MoS metrics negative, CRDO is priced for hyper-growth execution — and the data supports it: revenue growth of 206% YoY, 3Y CAGR of 94%, net margins expanding to 35%, near-monopoly in AEC (Active Electrical Cable) for AI clusters. The 52-week proximity of 69% reflects a strong but not fully extended run. Relative to MRVL (~40x FCF, lower growth rate) and ANET (~47x FCF, more mature), CRDO's premium is partly justified by its earlier growth stage but leaves little room for execution misses.
CRDO.rationale: Credo Technology Group (NASDAQ: CRDO), fabless semiconductor designer of high-speed SerDes, retimers, and active electrical cables for AI datacenter interconnect. Communication Equipment classification and 68% gross margin with 206% revenue growth confirm identity. Hyper-growth stage, genuinely differentiated in AEC market.

CTSH.relative_comps: Cognizant is a large-cap IT outsourcing and services company, structurally similar to Infosys and Wipro on the Indian IT services model. p_fcf of ~7.4x and multi-method MoS convergence (EPV +27%, owner earnings +49%, graham +22%) make CTSH cheap relative to its quality: ROIC of 14%, net margins of 11%, and gross margins of 34% are best-in-class among the Indian IT services peers on a per-dollar-of-services basis. Revenue growth of 7% YoY is modest but improving, and the 52-week proximity of 2% (near annual low) and below-SMA200 position signal continued selling pressure despite the value case. Relative to INFY (~18-22x FCF) and WIT (~20x FCF), CTSH's discount is notable and appears more than justified by any reasonable growth differential — this is genuinely cheap vs peers.
CTSH.rationale: Cognizant Technology Solutions (NASDAQ: CTSH), US-listed Indian-heritage IT services and outsourcing company. Information Technology Services classification, 34% gross margins, and 7% revenue growth confirm identity. Competes directly with INFY, WIT, HCL in IT outsourcing and digital transformation.

DAVE.relative_comps: Dave Inc. is a US neobank/fintech focused on cash advance, budgeting, and small-dollar lending to underbanked consumers. True peers are challenger banks and consumer fintech: SoFi (SOFI), Chime (private), and buy-now-pay-later adjacent AFRM and UPST on the credit-underwriting axis. At p_fcf of ~17x and iv15 MoS of +9% (the only positive signal), the valuation is moderate for a high-growth fintech: revenue growth of 60% YoY, 39% 3Y CAGR, net margins of 35%, and gross margins of 87% are exceptional for a consumer fintech at this scale. The 52-week proximity of 98% confirms the market has recently re-rated this sharply. Relative to SOFI (~40x FCF, slower growth, broader product mix) and UPST (~50x+ FCF), DAVE at 17x FCF with 60% growth and 35% net margins looks cheap in absolute terms, though limited MoS signals suggest the model struggles with its capex-light but credit-risk-heavy economics.
DAVE.rationale: Dave Inc. (NASDAQ: DAVE), US consumer neobank offering cash advances (ExtraCash), budgeting tools, and debit-card banking. Software-Application classification with 87% gross margins and 60% revenue growth confirm a software-economics fintech model. Competes with Chime, SoFi, and other challenger bank/fintech platforms.

EEFT.relative_comps: Euronet Worldwide is a payments infrastructure company operating ATM networks, electronic money transfers (Ria, epay), and point-of-sale systems primarily in emerging markets. True peers are WU (money transfer), FIS (payment processing), WEX (specialized payments), and FOUR (merchant acquiring). At p_fcf of ~7.8x with strong multi-method MoS (DCF +61%, graham +74%, iv15 +64%), EEFT is one of the clearest value propositions in its peer group. Gross margins of 41% and net margins of 7% reflect the ATM/transfer pass-through model; ROIC of 8% is modest but the FCF generation quality is strong. Revenue growth of 6% YoY is stable rather than exciting; the 52-week proximity of 17% and below-SMA200 position suggest the market has been pricing in secular headwinds from digital transfer disruption. Relative to WU (~10x FCF, lower growth) and FIS (~8x FCF), EEFT looks modestly cheap with better growth and emerging-market optionality.
EEFT.rationale: Euronet Worldwide (NASDAQ: EEFT), global payments infrastructure: ATM networks in Europe/CEE, Ria money transfer, and epay prepaid/digital-content distribution. Software-Infrastructure classification and 41% gross margins confirm the payments-platform model. Emerging-market ATM and remittance exposure distinguishes it from pure-play payment processors.

FIS.relative_comps: FIS (Fidelity National Information Services) is a large-cap banking-tech and payment processing company post the Worldpay divestiture (completed 2023), now focused on core banking software and financial institution tech. p_fcf of ~7.1x with positive DCF MoS (+45%) and EPV MoS (+16%) but negative graham and owner earnings signals confirm a mixed picture: the company is cheap on FCF yield but transformation execution risk is real. Gross margins of 37% and net margins of 4% are weak vs FISV (gross ~58%, net ~15%) and GPN (gross ~55%), reflecting the legacy hardware/services mix drag. Revenue growth of 5% YoY and 3% 3Y CAGR is slow. The 52-week proximity of 3% (near annual low) and below-SMA200 confirm sustained market skepticism post-Worldpay. Relative to FISV and JKHY, FIS is clearly cheaper on FCF multiples but the quality gap in margins and growth partially justifies the discount — cheap vs peers in multiple terms, but the quality gap is real.
FIS.rationale: Fidelity National Information Services (NYSE: FIS), banking-technology and payments platform serving financial institutions globally. Information Technology Services classification, 37% gross margins, and 5% revenue growth confirm identity post-Worldpay divestiture. Direct competitors are FISV (Fiserv), GPN (Global Payments), and JKHY (Jack Henry) in core banking and payment processing software.

HPQ.relative_comps: HP Inc. is a PC and printer hardware manufacturer competing with DELL, Lenovo, and to a lesser extent Apple in personal systems, and with Xerox/Lexmark in printing. p_fcf of ~7.8x and modest positive MoS signals (DCF +33%, owner earnings +43%) confirm a classic value-trap profile: cheap on FCF but declining secular end markets. Gross margins of 20% and net margins of 5% reflect hardware commodity economics; ROIC of 29% is high but driven by negative equity (asset-light after years of buybacks). Revenue growth of 3% YoY and a declining 3Y CAGR of -4% confirm structural headwinds. The 52-week proximity of 44% and modest SMA200 discount are neutral. Relative to DELL (~8x FCF, better datacenter growth) and Lenovo (lower multiple, higher volume), HPQ is in-line on valuation but faces the same secular PC/printer decline with less datacenter diversification than DELL.
HPQ.rationale: HP Inc. (NYSE: HPQ), personal systems (PCs, workstations) and printing (inkjet, laser) hardware company, separated from HPE in 2015. Computer Hardware classification and 20% gross margins confirm the commodity-hardware profile. Competes with DELL and Lenovo in PCs, Xerox in printing.

IOS.DE.relative_comps: IOS.DE is Intershop Communications AG (Frankfurt: IOS), a German B2B e-commerce platform software provider competing with SAP Commerce Cloud, Salesforce Commerce, Spryker, and commercetools in the European enterprise digital commerce space. p_fcf of ~12.4x and positive DCF MoS (+32%), graham (+57%), and owner earnings (+31%) suggest moderate cheapness for a small-cap software company. Gross margins of 57% and net margins of 17% with ROIC of 14% are healthy for a mid-market SaaS/software business. Revenue declined 16% YoY (likely a large contract roll-off or restructuring) vs positive 3Y CAGR of 1%, which creates uncertainty. The 52-week proximity of 25% confirms the market's skepticism. Relative to large enterprise commerce peers (SAP, CRM) that trade at 30-50x FCF, IOS.DE is cheap on absolute multiples, but the micro-cap scale and revenue volatility constrain the comparison — cheap in absolute terms but peer-set is largely private or much larger.
IOS.DE.rationale: Intershop Communications AG (FRA: IOS), German B2B e-commerce software platform for omnichannel retail and digital commerce. The .DE suffix and Software-Services classification with 57% gross margins and negative revenue growth confirm a small-cap European software company. NOT an iOS/Apple product or semiconductor.

LYFT.relative_comps: Lyft is a US-only rideshare duopolist competing almost exclusively with Uber (UBER); internationally, GRAB, DiDi, and Bolt are comparable but geographically non-overlapping. p_fcf of ~5.3x and positive graham MoS (+75%) with owner earnings MoS (+46%) make Lyft appear very cheap, but the low multiple partly reflects the structural second-place position vs UBER's global platform and diversified delivery revenue. Gross margins of 41% and net margins of 45% (the latter appears distorted by a one-time gain given the EPS YoY jump of 122x) show the business is FCF-positive at steady-state; ROIC of 8% is modest. Revenue growth of 9% YoY trails UBER's broader platform growth. The 52-week proximity of 14% and below-SMA200 position reflect persistent investor skepticism about the standalone rideshare model. Relative to UBER (~30x FCF, global platform premium), LYFT is structurally discounted but the gap is partly justified by lower defensibility.
LYFT.rationale: Lyft Inc. (NASDAQ: LYFT), US rideshare platform (rides only, no food delivery). Software-Application classification with 41% gross margins and 9% revenue growth confirm the tech-platform identity. The primary competitor is UBER; Lyft has no meaningful international operations.

MPWR.relative_comps: Monolithic Power Systems is a fabless analog and mixed-signal semiconductor company specializing in power management ICs for AI servers, EVs, and consumer electronics. True peers are TXN, ADI (broad analog), and focused power-management specialists like POWI and SLAB. At p_fcf of ~95x, all MoS metrics negative, MPWR is priced at a significant premium to TXN (~28x FCF) and ADI (~30x FCF). The premium is supported by superior growth (26% YoY vs TXN's near-flat) and expanding AI server content, but net margins of 22% and ROIC of 18% while strong are not structurally different from ADI or TXN. The 52-week proximity of 61% and SMA200 premium reflect the AI server power-management narrative. Relative to peers, MPWR trades 3-4x richer on FCF multiples; the growth premium accounts for some of this gap but not all — MPWR is rich vs the analog peer group.
MPWR.rationale: Monolithic Power Systems (NASDAQ: MPWR), fabless analog semiconductor company specializing in power management ICs. Semiconductors classification, 55% gross margins, and 26% revenue growth confirm identity. Competes with TXN and ADI in power management; trades at a large premium to those peers on AI server content.

MTSI.relative_comps: MACOM Technology Solutions (NASDAQ: MTSI) is a specialty RF, microwave, and millimeter-wave semiconductor company serving defense, datacenter, and telecom infrastructure. True peers are QRVO and SWKS (compound semiconductors/RF), MRVL (datacenter), and ADI (high-performance analog). At p_fcf of ~178x with all MoS metrics negative and negative net margins (-6%), MTSI is priced as a pure growth/optionality story: revenue growth of 33% YoY driven by datacenter PAM4 optical and defense radar wins. Gross margins of 55% are solid but net margins are suppressed by R&D and integration costs. The 52-week proximity of 83% and SMA200 premium confirm the AI/defense re-rating. Relative to QRVO (~15x FCF) and SWKS (~12x FCF, screened in this universe), MTSI's 178x multiple is extremely rich — justified only if the datacenter optical ramp sustains above-consensus revenue growth for several years.
MTSI.rationale: MACOM Technology Solutions (NASDAQ: MTSI), specialty compound semiconductor company (GaAs, InP, GaN) for RF/microwave, defense radar, and datacenter optical interconnect. Semiconductors classification, 55% gross margins, and 33% revenue growth confirm identity. The ticker MTSI unambiguously maps to MACOM (formerly M/A-COM Technology).

NEXI.MI.relative_comps: NEXI.MI is the Italian acquirer/payment processor (Nexi SpA), direct comparables are Worldline (delisted/restructured), Adyen, Global Payments, and within-universe WEX/FIS/EEFT. At p_fcf ~5x and DCF MoS of 52%, NEXI screens as the cheapest of any payments peer on FCF — Adyen trades at 50-70x FCF, Global Payments at 10-12x, FIS at ~7x. The gap to FIS/WEX is modest and partly justified by NEXI's negative reported net margin (Italian GAAP goodwill amortization inflates D&A, depressing IFRS net), but roic of only 1.9% and flat revenue (-0.3% YoY, 5-yr CAGR ~5%) vs Adyen's 20%+ growth make it value-not-momentum. Price sits at 29% of 52wk high and below SMA200 — momentum is negative, suggesting no rush, but the FCF yield is real.
NEXI.MI.rationale: NEXI.MI is Nexi SpA, Italy's largest payment processor (cards/POS/e-commerce acquiring). True peers are other European/global payment processors and payment software infrastructure players: Worldline, Adyen, FIS, Global Payments, WEX, EEFT. Not a software company — it is a capital-intensive acquirer with merchant-network economics and large goodwill from the SIA/Nets mergers.

NTES.relative_comps: NTES (NetEase) is a Chinese internet gaming and entertainment platform; true peers are Tencent, Bilibili, Sea Limited, Nexon, Capcom, and US game publishers EA/TTWO. At p_fcf ~12x and 30% net margin, NTES is dramatically cheaper than Western game publishers (EA ~20x FCF, TTWO loss-making) and modestly cheaper than Tencent (~15-18x). The 64% gross margin and 14% ROIC are strong for the cohort; the discount is partly China-risk/regulatory overhang and partly the absence of a major live-service franchise outside China. Revenue growth of only 7% YoY with 5% 3yr CAGR is below peers like Sea (+18%) but NTES generates real cash (no dilution, ~$6bn net cash). Sits at 33% of 52wk high, below SMA200 — some momentum impairment but valuation gap to Western peers is wide.
NTES.rationale: NTES is NetEase, China's second-largest online games company (PC/mobile: Fantasy Westward Journey, Naraka, Diablo Immortal). True peers are Chinese internet/gaming incumbents Tencent and Bilibili, plus global game publishers EA, Take-Two, Nexon, and Capcom. Classified as 'Electronic Gaming & Multimedia' — correct bucket.

NVDA.relative_comps: NVDA at p_fcf ~49x is expensive in absolute terms but sits mid-pack within the AI-accelerator and advanced-semiconductor cohort: AMD trades ~127x FCF (no GPU profit yet at scale), ARM at ~365x, ASML at ~61x. AVGO is the closest 'cashflow-rich compounder' comp at 66x FCF with lower growth (24% rev CAGR vs NVDA's 100%+ 3yr CAGR). NVDA's 56% net margin, 45% ROIC, and 65% YoY revenue growth this year are unmatched in semiconductors — only TSM's fab economics come close on margins. The p_fcf premium over peers is justified by monopoly-level positioning in GPU compute; the stock is at 48% of its 52wk range (well off highs) with an SMA200 at $190 vs current ~$143, suggesting technical consolidation.
NVDA.rationale: NVDA is NVIDIA, the dominant GPU/AI accelerator company. True peers are the semiconductor design duopoly (AMD) plus the IP licensor (ARM), advanced logic foundry (ASML upstream, TSM manufacturing), and diversified chip platforms (AVGO, QCOM, MRVL). Within-universe AMD and ASML are direct peers; AVGO and ARM also screened.

PLAB.relative_comps: PLAB (Photronics) makes photomasks — the glass plates used in semiconductor lithography. It is a niche capital-intensive supplier with effectively two global players (PLAB and Toppan Photomasks/DNP). There are no direct publicly-listed US comparables; true peers are Toppan Photomasks (subsidiary of Toppan Inc, Tokyo), Dai Nippon Printing's photomask division, and to a lesser extent specialty substrate makers. At p_fcf ~31x, PLAB looks rich vs its own 10-year average (~12x), revenue is down 2% YoY and flat 3yr CAGR (+1%), gross margin is 35% (lower than semiconductor peers). The high p_fcf reflects cyclical trough earnings rather than structural overvaluation — but ROIC of 11% and altman-z of 6.5 show a solid balance sheet. Momentum is weak (32% of 52wk range, SMA200 at $33.85 vs current ~$28).
PLAB.rationale: PLAB is Photronics Inc., the world's second-largest photomask supplier for semiconductor and FPD manufacturing. True peers are Toppan Photomasks (Japan, private subsidiary), Dai Nippon Printing's photomask unit, and other specialty lithography consumable suppliers. There are no other listed US pure-play photomask companies — PLAB has a quasi-duopoly with Toppan.

PRGS.relative_comps: PRGS (Progress Software) is a mid-market application development and data connectivity platform (OpenEdge, DataDirect, Telerik, MarkLogic). Closest peers are Pegasystems, Manhattan Associates, and within-universe VRSN (for durable niche software moat comparison). At p_fcf ~6.4x and 81% gross margin with DCF MoS ~37%, PRGS is cheap relative to its software peer group: Pega trades at 40-50x FCF, Manhattan at 35x+. The 30% YoY revenue growth is M&A-inflated (MarkLogic acquisition), underlying organic is mid-single digits. Net margin of 7.5% with 77-78% gross is lower than best-in-class, reflecting acquisition integration drag. PRGS sits at 22% of 52wk high and below SMA200 — momentum negative, but the FCF yield is genuinely attractive for a defensible niche platform.
PRGS.rationale: PRGS is Progress Software, a US-listed niche enterprise application platform (OpenEdge ERP runtime, Telerik UI components, MarkLogic data platform). True peers are Pegasystems, Manhattan Associates, smaller application software vendors with sticky mid-market bases. Not a general ERP (not SAP/Oracle territory) — it is a platform-as-runtime company with high switching costs.

RMBS.relative_comps: RMBS (Rambus) is a semiconductor IP licensing company focused on memory interface chips and security cores. True peers are CEVA (DSP/connectivity IP), Xperi (media IP), Dolby (media IP, listed), and to a lesser extent FORM (FormFactor, memory test). At p_fcf ~38x, RMBS looks rich vs FCF-generative IP businesses: Dolby trades ~25x FCF with higher margins (80%+ net), CEVA at 50x+ on much lower absolute FCF. RMBS's 76% gross margin and 33% net margin are top-tier for the IP cohort; 27% revenue growth and 16% 3yr CAGR reflect the HBM/DDR5 product cycle driving royalty ramp. The premium is arguably deserved given memory interface IP is a bottleneck in AI training systems (HBM4 ramp). Stock at 47% of 52wk range — mid-range momentum.
RMBS.rationale: RMBS is Rambus Inc., a semiconductor IP licensing company (memory interface controllers and PHY IP for DDR/HBM, plus security/cryptography cores). True peers are CEVA Inc. (wireless/AI IP), Xperi (entertainment IP), Dolby Laboratories (perceptual audio/imaging IP), and memory test equipment maker FormFactor. The IP licensing model means capex-light with high gross margins — not comparable to fabless chip designers.

SMTC.relative_comps: SMTC (Semtech) is a analog/mixed-signal semiconductor company known for LoRa IoT wireless technology and high-speed SerDes. True peers are Silicon Labs (SLAB), Skyworks (SWKS, in-universe), Qorvo (QRVO), and MPWR (Monolithic Power, in-universe Tech 1). At p_fcf ~83x with negative net margin (-3.8%), Semtech is expensive and not yet recovered from the Sierra Wireless acquisition digestion/write-downs. SWKS at 9.5x FCF with 12% net margin is dramatically cheaper for a similar analog/RF exposure; SLAB/QRVO trade at 20-40x on recovery earnings. SMTC's LoRa IoT franchise has genuine network-effect value but the leverage from the acquisition and negative current earnings means it is priced for a full recovery that has not arrived. Stock at 80% of 52wk high — strong momentum but fundamentals lag the price.
SMTC.rationale: SMTC is Semtech Corporation, a analog/mixed-signal and IoT semiconductor company (LoRa/LoRaWAN LPWAN chipsets, high-speed SerDes for data center, Sierra Wireless cellular IoT). True peers are Skyworks Solutions (SWKS, in-universe), Qorvo, Silicon Labs, and MPWR for the mixed-signal/analog dimension. LoRa IoT gives SMTC a unique wireless-protocol moat but the Sierra Wireless acquisition created significant leverage.

SWKS.relative_comps: SWKS (Skyworks Solutions) supplies RF front-end modules and mixed-signal semiconductors, primarily to Apple (historically ~60% of revenue) and Android OEMs. True peers are Qorvo, Cirrus Logic, and Broadcom's wireless division (AVGO). At p_fcf ~9.5x with DCF MoS 53%, SWKS is cheap relative to peer Qorvo (~15x FCF) and Broadcom (~66x), but the discount is justified by structural headwinds: Apple in-sourcing RF components and rising competition from RFMD/Qorvo in Android. Revenue declined 2% YoY and -9% 3yr CAGR — a secular revenue shrinkage story in a commoditizing end-market. Gross margin of 41% is the lowest in the RF semi cohort (Cirrus Logic ~52%, Qorvo ~45%). Stock at 41% of 52wk range with SMA200 at $66 — modest momentum. The valuation is cheap but the cheapness may be structural.
SWKS.rationale: SWKS is Skyworks Solutions, a RF front-end and mixed-signal semiconductor supplier for mobile and IoT devices. True peers are Qorvo (direct RF comp), Cirrus Logic (audio/haptic ICs for Apple), Broadcom wireless (AVGO, in-universe), and to a lesser extent ADI for mixed-signal breadth. The Apple concentration risk and in-sourcing threat define the relative narrative.

SWON.SW.relative_comps: SWON.SW is Swisscom AG, the Swiss incumbent telecom and technology distributor (telecom services + IT distribution). True peers are Deutsche Telekom, Vodafone, Tele2, Orange, and Italian TIM — all incumbent European telcos. At p_fcf ~5.2x with EPV MoS of 60%, SWON screens as one of the cheapest European telcos on a cash-flow basis; DT trades ~10x FCF, Vodafone ~8x. However, SWON's 0.07% net margin is alarming — the 'Technology Distributor' classification (SIC/FMP) likely reflects low-margin IT resale business dragging reported margins. Revenue grew 22% YoY (likely IT distribution inclusion) vs Swisscom's actual telecom recurring revenue growth of ~2-3%. Altman-z of 0.63 flags elevated leverage typical of telcos. Stock at 53% of 52wk range — neutral momentum.
SWON.SW.rationale: SWON.SW is Swisscom AG (SWX: SCMN), Switzerland's dominant integrated telecom operator with fiber broadband, mobile, and IT services. The 'Technology Distributor' industry tag from FMP is a mis-classification artifact — Swisscom's core is regulated telco infrastructure. True peers are the European incumbent telcos: Deutsche Telekom, Vodafone, Orange, Tele2, and Swisscom's Italian subsidiary Fastweb parent.

UPBD.relative_comps: UPBD (Upbound Group, formerly Rent-A-Center) operates rent-to-own retail and the Acima virtual lease-to-own fintech platform. True peers are Aaron's Holdings (AAN, recently merged with Progressive Leasing to form PRG, also in-universe), RCII's split-off legacy, and subprime consumer finance players OMF (OneMain), CACC (Credit Acceptance). At p_fcf ~5x with owner_earnings MoS of 91% and EPV MoS of 83%, UPBD is one of the cheapest names on the screener on a multi-method basis. The 34% gross margin and 1.6% net margin reflect the capital cost of leased merchandise, but the 86% revenue growth YoY (Acima rapid scaling) is high. Revenue CAGR of 3.4% (3yr) is more muted suggesting recent acceleration. Priced near 38% of 52wk range with altman-z of 2.95 — reasonable leverage. The cheapness appears real but the subprime consumer credit-quality risk is the key overhang.
UPBD.rationale: UPBD is Upbound Group (formerly Rent-A-Center), a US rent-to-own and virtual lease-to-own platform. True peers are Aaron's/PRG (also in-universe Industrials group as PRG), Marlin Leasing, OneMain Financial, and subprime consumer lenders like World Acceptance. The Acima virtual LTO platform competes with Katapult, Flexiti, and other embedded fintech lease options.

VNT.relative_comps: VNT (Vontier) is a Fortive spinoff providing fuel/EV dispenser technology, fleet telematics, and environmental compliance hardware/software. True peers are Dover's fueling segment (competitor Gilbarco Veeder-Root's parent historically), Trimble (precision tech), and Fortive (FTV) itself. At p_fcf ~10x with owner_earnings MoS 57%, VNT is cheap relative to FTV (~22x FCF) and Trimble (~25x FCF). The 47% gross margin, 13% net margin, and 12.8% ROIC are respectable for an industrial-tech spin. Revenue growth is muted (3% YoY, -1% 3yr CAGR) reflecting the mature fuel retail end-market, partially offset by EV charging station management growth. Stock at only 11% of 52wk range and well below SMA200 — strong negative price momentum. Valuation is genuinely cheap vs FTV but the low-growth/ex-growth narrative is partly priced in.
VNT.rationale: VNT is Vontier Corporation, a technology/hardware/software company serving transportation and mobility end-markets (fuel dispensers, EV charging management, fleet telematics, environmental compliance). Spun from Fortive in 2020. True peers are Fortive (FTV, the parent), Dover Corporation's Fueling Solutions segment (which competes directly with Gilbarco), and Trimble for the precision/telematics dimension.

VRSN.relative_comps: VRSN (VeriSign) is the ICANN-designated registry operator for .com and .net domains — a regulated monopoly with price-increase authority. True peers are essentially non-existent as true registry monopolies: the closest are GoDaddy (registrar/downstream, not registry), Tucows (registrar), and Nominet (UK, private). At p_fcf ~22x, VRSN looks in-line with its own history (~18-25x), and its 88% gross margin and 50% net margin are best-in-class. ROIC is reported as -122% due to negative book equity (share buybacks have eliminated equity entirely), which is a financial engineering artifact, not operational weakness. Revenue growth of 6% YoY and 5% 3yr CAGR matches domain pricing increases (contractually allowed +7%/yr). Stock at 45% of 52wk range, SMA200 at $258 — neutral to mild negative momentum. The premium vs average software is justified by the monopoly contract, but upside is limited to buyback yield + 6% growth.
VRSN.rationale: VRSN is VeriSign Inc., the exclusive ICANN-designated registry for .com and .net top-level domains. It is a regulated utility monopoly with no true public peer — the closest comparables are GoDaddy (registrar, downstream, not registry), Tucows (multi-service registrar), and EEFT (also in-universe, for non-bank payments infrastructure comparison). The negative ROIC is a buyback artifact (negative book equity), not economic impairment.

WEX.relative_comps: WEX Inc. provides fleet payment cards, corporate travel payment infrastructure, and healthcare payment accounts (HSA/FSA/HRA). True peers are Fleetcor (now Corpay, CPAY), Nuvei, Paya Holdings (acquired), and within-universe FIS and EEFT. At p_fcf ~16x, WEX is in-line to slightly cheap vs Corpay (~18x FCF with comparable margins). Both companies are toll-road-like B2B payment networks with 50%+ gross margins. WEX's 55% gross, 11% net, and 6.5% ROIC are slightly below Corpay (70% gross, 35% net), reflecting WEX's healthcare payments segment drag and higher leverage from acquisitions. Revenue growth is near-flat (1% YoY, 4% 3yr CAGR) vs Corpay's mid-single-digit organic — modest relative headwind. Stock at only 21% of 52wk range with SMA200 at $153 — bearish momentum, suggesting re-rating risk. Moderately cheap but momentum overhang is real.
WEX.rationale: WEX Inc. is a US-listed B2B payments platform serving fleet/corporate cards, travel & expense, and healthcare payment accounts. True peers are Corpay (CPAY, formerly Fleetcor) as the closest direct fleet-payments analog, plus FIS (in-universe) for payment infrastructure comparison, and EEFT (in-universe) for specialty payment networks. Not a consumer payments company.

AD.AS.relative_comps: Ahold Delhaize (Albert Heijn/Stop & Shop/Food Lion) trades at p_fcf ~4.3x, a notable discount to US peer Kroger (~10-12x p_fcf) and inline with French rival Carrefour (CA.PA, p_fcf ~5.1x), reflecting European grocery margin compression and the structural drag from its US Stop & Shop turnaround. Its gross margin of 26.5% compares favorably to Carrefour (16.6%) but lags the higher-mix US formats; ROIC of 7.3% is modest but acceptable for a capital-intensive food retailer. The DCF/FCF MoS of 0.60 is strong, suggesting genuine undervaluation relative to normalized free cash flow, though the stock is sitting near the lower third of its 52-week range (proximity ~0.32), consistent with near-term macro headwinds. The gap vs US grocers is partly justified by lower US market position and European regulatory/competitive environment, but the discount looks excessive versus the quality of the Dutch and Belgian banners.
AD.AS.rationale: Ahold Delhaize is a large-cap pan-Atlantic grocery operator; true peers are Carrefour (CA.PA), Colruyt (COLR.BR), Jeronimo Martins (JMT.LS), Kroger (KR), and Walmart (WMT) for scale context. Morrison/Marks (UK) and Dollar General (DG) for format comparison. Non-European peers command structural P/FCF premiums the screener data does not reflect.

BRBR.relative_comps: BellRing Brands (Premier Protein, Dymatize) is a high-growth active nutrition play; its p_fcf of ~5.8x looks very cheap headline but masks a business whose revenue CAGR of ~19% and ROIC of 36.7% are exceptional for the packaged food space — far ahead of Post Holdings (POST, ~8-9x p_fcf, ~8% growth) or Simply Good Foods (SMPL, ~18x, ~12% growth). The graham MoS (0.77) and IV15 MoS (0.82) signal the model finds deep value here. The stock sits near its 52-week low (proximity 0.07), which combined with strong fundamentals points to a potential sentiment dislocation rather than a structural problem. The eps_yoy decline (-9.7%) is likely an investment/share-comp artifact rather than an economic impairment given the ROIC profile.
BRBR.rationale: BellRing competes directly with Simply Good Foods (SMPL), Post Holdings (POST, parent), Celsius Holdings (CELH, energy/nutrition), Nomad Foods (NOMD) for shelf positioning, and indirectly with General Mills (GIS) and Campbell's (CPB) in broader packaged food. Its growth rate and ROIC profile justify a significant premium to slow-growth CPG peers, yet it screens cheaper on p_fcf than most — a real mispricing candidate.

CA.PA.relative_comps: Carrefour (French/international hypermarket operator) trades at p_fcf ~5.1x with a DCF MoS of 0.59 and owner-earnings MoS of 0.51 — broadly similar valuation to Ahold Delhaize (AD.AS, p_fcf 4.3x) but with meaningfully inferior economics: gross margin 16.6% vs AD.AS 26.5%, net margin 0.38% vs AD.AS 2.4%, ROIC 4.5% vs AD.AS 7.3%. Carrefour is structurally a lower-quality compounder — hyper exposure, heavy LatAm/EM footprint, and weak pricing power in France vs discounters (Lidl/Aldi). The 52wk proximity of 0.80 (near high) is surprising given the eps_yoy of -56%, likely reflecting a bounce from depressed lows; the low multiple is justified by low-quality earnings not mispricing.
CA.PA.rationale: Carrefour's true peers are Ahold Delhaize (AD.AS), Colruyt (COLR.BR), Jeronimo Martins (JMT.LS), Casino (unlisted/restructured), Tesco (TSCO.L), and Ocado (OCDO.L) for digital grocery. The discount vs AD.AS is fully explained by inferior margin structure and capital intensity; cheap on FCF multiples but not a quality mispricing.

CALM.relative_comps: Cal-Maine Foods is the dominant US shell-egg producer — a commodity business with enormous earnings cyclicality driven by avian flu-induced supply shocks. Its p_fcf of 3.67x and near-perfect MoS scores across DCF (0.67), EPV (0.56), and owner-earnings (0.65) reflect the current peak of the egg-price supercycle (rev_yoy +83%, net_margin 28.6%, roic 20.9%) — numbers that are not sustainable at normalized egg prices. True peers are Pilgrim's Pride (PPC, poultry proteins), Tyson (TSN), and Hormel (HRL), though egg is structurally more volatile. The stock at proximity_52wk 0.15 (already well off highs) signals the market is pricing in mean-reversion correctly; the screener MoS reflects backward-looking peak earnings, not forward normalized FCF. Rich vs true mid-cycle value despite the low headline multiple.
CALM.rationale: Cal-Maine's closest peers are commodity protein processors: Pilgrim's Pride (PPC), Tyson Foods (TSN), Hormel (HRL), and historically Sanderson Farms (SAFM, now merged into Wayne-Sanderson). The egg-price cycle is the dominant driver; at normalized prices (~$1.50-2/doz vs current $4+), earnings collapse 70-80%, making current multiples misleading. PPC at p_fcf 10x with more stable economics is a better quality comp.

INGR.relative_comps: Ingredion is a B2B specialty ingredient supplier (starches, sweeteners, texturizers) competing with Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) and Bunge (BG) at commodity scale but with IFF and Tate & Lyle (TATE.L) on specialty. Its p_fcf of 12.4x and gross_margin of 25.3% place it between the commodity traders (ADM ~8-10x, ~6% gross) and pure specialty players (IFF ~15x, ~35% gross). ROIC of 9% and decelerating revenue (-2.8% yoy, -3.1% CAGR) suggest mix-shift headwinds; the stock sits at proximity_52wk 0.022, essentially at a 52-week low, while the graham MoS (0.72) and DCF MoS (0.45) suggest fair-to-cheap pricing. The discount appears partially justified by the revenue contraction but the multi-method convergence methodology flags it as genuinely cheap on normalized earnings.
INGR.rationale: Ingredion's business model — converting corn/tapioca into B2B food ingredients — overlaps most directly with Tate & Lyle (TATE.L, UK), ADM's ingredient division, Bunge (BG), and specialty peers IFF (flavor/ingredient) and Givaudan (GIVN.SW). The stock is near 52-week lows with stable margins, suggesting an indiscriminate sector sell-off rather than a fundamental deterioration.

PPC.relative_comps: Pilgrim's Pride is a vertically integrated chicken processor (JBS subsidiary) competing with Tyson Foods (TSN) and Hormel (HRL) in US protein. Its p_fcf of 10.3x is in-line with Tyson (~9-11x) and a premium to the screener universe, but justified by its more stable chicken-focused model vs Tyson's diversified beef/pork exposure. Gross margin of 12.7% is typical for poultry processing (thin-margin commodity business), net margin 5.9% is near-normal. Revenue growth (+3.5% yoy) is modest and eps_yoy flat (-0.7%), consistent with a post-supercycle normalization in chicken prices. The stock sits near its 52wk low (proximity 0.088), with a Graham MoS of 0.76 suggesting it screens as statistically cheap relative to earnings power, though the business quality is structurally limited by its protein commodity exposure and JBS ownership overhang.
PPC.rationale: PPC's true peers are Tyson Foods (TSN), Hormel (HRL), Wayne-Sanderson (private, formerly Sanderson Farms/SAFM), and WH Group (HK:288, global pork/chicken). The JBS parent ownership creates a discount vs standalone peers; at current p_fcf it is neither notably cheap nor expensive for a poultry commodity processor at mid-cycle.

SPB.relative_comps: Spectrum Brands is a branded household products conglomerate (HHI hardware, Remington, Black+Decker licenses, pet, home & garden) whose p_fcf of 13.7x is elevated given its revenue contraction (-5.2% yoy, -3.6% CAGR) and near-zero ROIC (0.23%), suggesting significant capital misallocation or restructuring headwinds. The Graham MoS of 0.95 and owner-earnings MoS of 0.75 are striking but rely on asset values or normalized earnings, not current cash generation — dcf_fcff_mos and epv_mos are both -1.0 (no value on those methods), which signals the earnings quality is poor. True peers Church & Dwight (CHD, ~24x p_fcf, 20% ROIC) and Energizer (ENR, ~8x, 15% ROIC) command either a clear quality premium or value-trap discount respectively. SPB at near-52wk-high (proximity 0.98) with declining revenues and negligible ROIC looks priced for a recovery that has not yet materialized.
SPB.rationale: Spectrum Brands competes with Church & Dwight (CHD), Energizer Holdings (ENR), Edgewell Personal Care (EPC), Newell Brands (NWL), and private Henkel/HPC in home, hardware, pet, and personal care. The asset-heavy conglomerate discount is appropriate given the ROIC profile; Graham MoS flatters a restructuring story, and the near-52wk-high price with deteriorating fundamentals makes this the most concerning name in the group.

AMR.relative_comps: AMR is a pure-play high-vol metallurgical (coking) coal producer — peers are HCC (Warrior Met Coal), CEIX (CONSOL/Arch merged entity), BTU (Peabody, mixed met/thermal), and internationally Teck Resources and Coronado Global. On valuation, AMR's p_fcf of ~121x looks optically extreme, but is distorted by a transitory negative net margin (-2.9%) on severely compressed revenues (-28% YoY, -20% 3Y CAGR) as met coal prices have collapsed from 2022 highs; DCF and owner-earnings MoS (0.73/0.64) suggest the asset base still carries substantial replacement value. Historical ROIC of 28% reflects the exceptional 2021-2022 super-cycle earnings, not the current trough; vs. HCC (~8-10x EV/EBITDA mid-cycle) and BTU (p_fcf ~5x on current trough cash flows), AMR screens rich on near-term earnings but in-line to cheap on normalised mid-cycle multiples. Gross margin of 0.13% (near-zero) is a trough artifact — met coal economics are highly operating-leveraged to benchmark prices. Momentum is neutral: price sits ~41% from 52-wk high, just below SMA200, consistent with sector-wide de-rating on China steel demand concerns.
AMR.rationale: AMR's true peers are met-coal specialists (HCC, CEIX, BTU, TECK's steelmaking coal segment, Coronado), NOT thermal-coal peers like ARLP. Current near-zero margins and negative revenue trend are driven by the post-2022 met coal price cycle, not structural deterioration; mid-cycle asset value (DCF MoS 0.73) keeps it in-line rather than outright cheap given the commodity timing risk.

SSRM.relative_comps: SSRM (SSR Mining) is a mid-tier gold/silver producer whose peer set is KGC (Kinross), EQX (Equinox Gold), PAAS (Pan American Silver), NGD (New Gold), CDE (Coeur Mining), and AG (First Majestic). Revenue surged +67% YoY — largely a base-effect recovery following the February 2024 Copler mine pit collapse (Turkey) that destroyed ~35% of production; net margin of 24% and graham MoS of 0.77 look attractive, but ROIC of only 2.2% exposes the capital-intensity drag of rebuilding/replacing that asset. p_fcf of ~26x is elevated vs. KGC (~12-15x) and PAAS (~18x) on current consensus, suggesting the market is pricing in a recovery to normalised multi-asset production rather than today's impaired base. Vs. peers, SSRM screens rich-to-in-line on current-year FCF multiples but justified if Copler remediation proceeds; proximity_52wk of 0.71 reflects gold-price tailwinds in 2025 partially offsetting the operational discount. Margins and growth trajectory are operationally below mid-tier peer averages given single-asset concentration risk post-Copler.
SSRM.rationale: SSRM's true peers are mid-tier gold/silver producers (KGC, EQX, PAAS, NGD, CDE); the stock screens modestly rich on current-year FCF multiples vs. this peer set because the 2024 Copler disaster artificially inflated the revenue YoY base while ROIC (2.2%) remains well below peers (KGC ~7%, PAAS ~5%), reflecting ongoing capital drag from remediation; the graham MoS looks compelling but is based on book/Graham formula which rewards gold-asset book values — the FCF-based valuation gap to peers is the more honest signal.

A2A.MI.relative_comps: A2A SpA is a Milan-listed Italian multi-utility (electricity generation + distribution, gas distribution, district heating, water, and waste/circular economy), directly comparable to Hera (HER.MI, p_fcf ~12-14x, ROIC ~7-8%) and Iren (IRE.MI, p_fcf ~10-13x) — both Italian multi-utilities with similar regulated/quasi-regulated revenue mixes. A2A trades at p_fcf 7.0x versus these peers, a 30-50% discount, with a Graham MoS of 84% — the cheapest in the Italian utility cohort on a cash-flow basis. Margins are comparably thin (gross 11.4%, net 5.5%), reflecting capital-intensive Italian network regulation, consistent with Hera and Iren. ROIC of 5.2% is in-line with sector norms for regulated Italian utilities (Hera ~6-8%, Iren ~5-7%), so the quality gap does not justify the valuation discount. Growth is muted (revenue +9.3% YoY but -15.7% 3yr CAGR, distorted by 2022 energy-price spike reversal; EPS CAGR +10.7% five-year is decent) and FCF generation is recovering sharply (+131% YoY). The stock sits 31% up the 52-week range and 4% below its 200d SMA, suggesting technical neutrality. Versus large-cap European integrated utilities (E.ON, RWE), A2A is a smaller, more Italian-specific, higher-political-risk asset, but the valuation discount at p_fcf 7x vs E.ON/RWE at 10-16x EV/EBITDA equivalents is wide enough to price in that risk and then some. The discount looks like a real mispricing relative to direct Italian peers rather than a justified quality gap — the owner_earnings MoS of +16% confirms a floor even on conservative assumptions.
A2A.MI.rationale: A2A SpA (A2A.MI) is a Milan-listed Italian diversified utility operating electricity generation, electricity and gas distribution networks, district heating, water services, and waste/circular economy businesses. Its true peers are the other Italian multi-utilities Hera (HER.MI) and Iren (IRE.MI), plus ENEL for electricity scale, TERNA for Italian grid infrastructure, and large-cap European integrated utilities E.ON (E.DE) and RWE (RWE.DE) for cross-border context. The .MI suffix confirms this is the Italian Borsa Italiana listing — there is no identity ambiguity. A2A trades at a 30-50% p_fcf discount to its closest Italian peers (Hera, Iren) with broadly similar ROIC and margin profiles, and no structural quality deficit that would justify the gap; the Graham MoS of 84% and recovering FCF trajectory support a cheap_vs_peers verdict.

AEO.relative_comps: AEO trades at ~16x P/FCF with a graham_mos of 0.74 — cheap on a normalized-earnings basis vs ANF (11.7x, higher quality) and URBN (~15x), but the quality gap is real: AEO's gross margin (33%) trails ANF's 61% by a wide margin, reflecting AEO's greater reliance on promotions and lower brand pricing power. Revenue growth (+3.2% YoY, +3.3% 3Y CAGR) is materially slower than ANF's +6.4%/+12.5% and URBN's mid-single-digit growth, while EPS fell 35% YoY — suggesting margin compression rather than structural mispricing. The discount is partially justified: AEO carries a promotional-retail business model with thin margins and no compelling brand moat, so the cheap FCF multiple reflects execution risk rather than classic undervaluation.
AEO.rationale: AEO is a US teen/young-adult apparel retailer (American Eagle + Aerie) directly competing with ANF, URBN (Urban Outfitters/Free People), GAP brands (GPS), and Express; internationally with ASOS and H&M. These are the true comparables by end-market (15-30yr consumer), channel (mall/DTC), and capital intensity (lease-heavy, inventory-driven).

ANF.relative_comps: ANF is the standout quality name in specialty apparel: 61% gross margin vs AEO's 33% and URBN's ~33%, 14.4% ROIC vs AEO's 9.3%, and +12.5% 3Y revenue CAGR driven by the Abercrombie brand reinvention and Hollister recovery. At 11.7x P/FCF with a DCF MoS of 0.47 and owner-earnings MoS of 0.49, it screens materially cheaper than the quality warrants — trading near the bottom third of its 52-week range (proximity=0.39). The premium to AEO is fully justified by margins and growth; the gap to European fast-fashion giants (ITX, H&M) that trade at 20-25x reflects ANF's perceived cyclicality despite structurally improved brand positioning.
ANF.rationale: ANF (Abercrombie & Fitch / Hollister) competes directly with AEO, Urban Outfitters, GAP/Old Navy, PVH (Calvin Klein/Tommy), Ralph Lauren at the aspirational tier, and internationally with Zara/Inditex and H&M. These peers share the same 18-35 consumer demographic, multi-channel distribution, and gross-margin-driven economics.

BALL.relative_comps: BALL trades at ~21.6x P/FCF — rich vs GEF (no reported FCF, industrial containers) and in-line with Crown Holdings (CCK ~18-20x) and Silgan (SLGN ~15x). The premium is partially justified by BALL's aluminum beverage can franchise (higher margin, better ESG narrative) vs GEF's industrial drums and SLGN's food cans, but the 3Y revenue CAGR of -4.9% and EPS decline of 75% YoY reflect the Aerospace divestiture reshaping and volume headwinds in North America. At 14.9% gross margin and 6.7% ROIC, BALL's returns are modest for the multiple; the stock sits at 72% of its 52-week high, suggesting the market is pricing in a recovery that has yet to materialize in the numbers.
BALL.rationale: BALL is an aluminum beverage can and aerosol packaging manufacturer — its true peers are Crown Holdings (CCK), Silgan Holdings (SLGN), Ardagh Metal Packaging (ARD.DE), Amcor (AMCR), and on the industrial side Greif (GEF) and Ds Smith (DS.PA). The competitive moat is scale and long-term customer contracts, not brand, making valuation multiple and ROIC the key relative benchmarks.

BBWI.relative_comps: BBWI stands out for its 25.5% ROIC — highest in the consumer cyclical group — reflecting the franchise strength of its body-care/candle format and the absence of a wholesale drag. At 5.6x P/FCF with convergence MoS support and 43.7% gross margins, it trades at a deep discount to EL (~30x) and ULTA (~20x) while delivering comparable or superior returns on capital. Revenue is flat (-0.2% YoY) and EPS fell 15%, but this reflects post-spin normalization rather than structural decay; the 44% 52-week position and near-SMA200 price imply the market is pricing ongoing weakness rather than the underlying cash generation, making this a genuine relative mispricing vs beauty/personal-care peers.
BBWI.rationale: BBWI (Bath & Body Works) operates a specialty beauty/personal-care retail franchise (soaps, lotions, candles) competing with Estee Lauder brands at mass, ULTA's private label, and Target/Amazon house brands at the value end; its closest structural analog is a high-ROIC specialty retailer with consumable repurchase dynamics, not apparel. True peers are EL, ULTA, IFF (fragrance supply chain), and specialty beauty retailers.

BKNG.relative_comps: BKNG commands a 32.7% ROIC and 20% net margins — the highest quality profile in its peer set — at 16.3x P/FCF, in-line with EXPE (~14-16x) but at a premium to TCOM (~12x). The premium is fully justified: BKNG's asset-light model (gross margin=100%, pure-take-rate), European OTA dominance via Booking.com, and superior free-cash-flow conversion make it structurally superior to EXPE (lower margins, higher capital intensity) and ABNB (still investing in growth). At 38% of its 52-week range and near recent lows, the market appears to be discounting macro travel risk; on a quality-adjusted basis this is the cheapest it has traded relative to peers in several years.
BKNG.rationale: BKNG (Booking Holdings — Booking.com, Priceline, Kayak, Agoda) is an online travel agency (OTA) directly competing with Expedia Group (EXPE), Airbnb (ABNB), Trip.com (TCOM), TripAdvisor (TRIP), and smaller European OTAs. The business economics are take-rate on gross bookings — 100% software gross margins, zero inventory — making EXPE, ABNB, and TCOM the correct relative-value anchors.

BYD.relative_comps: BYD (Boyd Gaming — US regional casino operator) trades at 18.9x P/FCF with 42.1% gross margins and a 12.2% ROIC, broadly in-line with RRR (Red Rock Resorts, closest regional peer at ~16-18x) but cheaper than LVS/WYNN (~20-25x) which benefit from Macau/Singapore premium. The EPS surge (+265% YoY) is striking but likely reflects a one-time item or comps effect; at 97% of its 52-week high the market is already pricing strong results. BYD's regional casino model (no Macau, no Singapore) caps the growth premium; on a pure FCF basis relative to CZR and PENN it screens in-line to slightly rich given the already-elevated 52-week position.
BYD.rationale: BYD is Boyd Gaming, a US regional casino operator (Nevada, Midwest, South) — its true peers are other regional gaming operators: Red Rock Resorts (RRR), Penn Entertainment (PENN), Caesars Entertainment (CZR), Monarch Casino (MCRI), and to a lesser degree the Las Vegas Strip operators MGM/LVS/WYNN. Business model is slot/table floor operations with hotel/F&B ancillary, high operating leverage, capital-intensive.

CARG.relative_comps: CARG (CarGurus — online automotive marketplace) carries an 88.9% gross margin and 16.6% net margin at 11x P/FCF, superficially cheap, but the 3Y revenue CAGR of -17.2% reveals significant volume decay, likely from its pivot away from low-margin digital wholesale (CarOffer wind-down). The DCF MoS of -0.06 implies fair value near current price, but the near-SMA200 position (51% 52-week) and modest revenue trajectory suggest the market is correctly pricing a business in transition. Relative to CARS.COM (a true marketplace analog) and KMX (a dealer/retailer), CARG's high-margin marketplace model is superior structurally but the growth investment cycle and customer concentration in franchised dealers create execution risk.
CARG.rationale: CARG (CarGurus) is an online automotive listings and marketplace platform — its true peers are Cars.com (CARS), TrueCar (TRUE), AutoTrader (private), and Autobytel; in the broader auto-retail digital ecosystem it competes for dealer budgets alongside KMX (CarMax), AN (AutoNation), and LAD (Lithia). The business model is advertising/subscription to dealers, not physical car sales, making high-gross-margin software/media peers the right valuation anchor.

CEC.DE.relative_comps: CEC.DE (Ceconomy AG — MediaMarkt/Saturn, Europe's largest consumer electronics retailer) trades at just 2.4x P/FCF — the cheapest FCF multiple in the consumer cyclical group — with multiple MoS signals positive (DCF 0.82, EPV 0.64, owner-earnings 0.79). The 15.1% gross margin and near-zero net margin (-0.15%) reflect the structural reality of a big-box electronics retailer competing against Amazon/online: thin margins, lease obligations, and volume sensitivity. At 18% of its 52-week range the stock is severely depressed; relative to Currys (UK, similar model) and FNAC Darty (France, more mixed-retail), CEC looks statistically cheapest but the quality gap is real — ROIC at 1.8% barely covers cost of capital, and the business faces secular headwinds from e-commerce. The valuation discount is more value-trap than mispricing.
CEC.DE.rationale: CEC.DE is Ceconomy AG, the German-listed holding company of MediaMarkt and Saturn consumer electronics chains, the dominant European big-box CE retailer. True peers are Currys plc (CURY.L, UK), Fnac Darty (FNAC.PA, France), Best Buy (BBY, US equivalent), and specialty electronics retailers. The model is physical/omnichannel CE retail — high volume, thin margins, capital-intensive leases.

CHWY.relative_comps: CHWY (Chewy — online pet supplies/food) trades at 14x P/FCF with only 29.8% gross margin and a 1.8% net margin, near the bottom of its 52-week range (4% proximity). Revenue growth of +6.2% YoY and +7.6% 3Y CAGR is respectable for a maturing e-commerce platform, but 3.9% ROIC is thin given the capital and working capital intensity. Relative to PetSmart (private) and the Petco/WOOF peer, CHWY commands a premium for its autoship-subscription stickiness and vet-telehealth optionality, but the negative owner-earnings MoS (-0.93) suggests normalized free cash generation is weaker than reported. Near 52-week lows vs strong revenue growth is a flag — either an emerging entry point or a sign the market sees a structural margin problem.
CHWY.rationale: CHWY competes directly in online pet supplies/food against Amazon (dominant), Petco (WOOF, public), PetSmart (private, owned by BC Partners), and Walmart/Target in the mass channel. Its autoship model gives it subscription-like retention dynamics, and the true peer set for valuation is specialty e-commerce with high repeat-purchase economics and vet-care adjacency.

CROX.relative_comps: CROX (Crocs + HeyDude) at 10.5x P/FCF with 57% gross margins and 18.7% ROIC — essentially the same quality profile as DECK — screens materially cheaper than DECK (13.9x) and far cheaper than ON Running (ONON, 40x+). The negative net margin (-2%) is anomalous given strong gross margins and likely reflects a HeyDude impairment or one-time charge. At 96% of its 52-week high the stock has recovered strongly despite the headline loss, suggesting the market is looking through the EPS distortion to the core FCF. The discount to DECK appears unjustified on quality metrics (ROIC 18.7% vs DECK 28.3% is the key gap) but CROX trades cheap because investors discount the HeyDude integration risk and question whether Crocs' brand can sustain its premium post-pandemic novelty cycle.
CROX.rationale: CROX (Crocs footwear + HeyDude) competes in casual/comfort footwear against Deckers Outdoor (DECK — UGG/HOKA), Birkenstock (BIRK.DE), On Running (ONON), Skechers (SKX), and Steve Madden (SHOO). These brands share the DTC/wholesale hybrid model, high gross margins, and brand-premium economics; the key differentiator is brand lifecycle trajectory and international growth optionality.

DECK.relative_comps: DECK (Deckers — HOKA + UGG) combines the highest ROIC in footwear (28.3%) with 56% gross margins, 18.8% net margin, and +14.6% 3Y revenue CAGR — a premium business at a mid-premium multiple (13.9x P/FCF, convergence MoS positive). HOKA's performance running growth is the key driver; relative to ONON (~40-50x P/FCF) DECK is dramatically cheaper for similar growth trajectory, and relative to CROX (10.5x, lower ROIC) DECK's modest premium is entirely justified. At 54% of its 52-week range with multiple valuation methods confirming margin of safety, DECK is the strongest risk-adjusted name in the footwear peer set — in-line valuation that leans slightly cheap given the HOKA brand momentum.
DECK.rationale: DECK competes in performance and lifestyle footwear via HOKA (running, trail) against On Running (ONON), Nike (NKE), New Balance (private), and via UGG (fashion/sheepskin boots) against Ugg-alternatives and fashion footwear (SHOO, BIRK). The combined portfolio gives DECK unusual exposure to both performance and lifestyle end-markets — true peers span the premium athletic and casualwear footwear universe.

DFH.relative_comps: DFH (Dream Finders Homes — small-cap 'asset-light' homebuilder) at 0x P/FCF (typical for homebuilders who consume cash in land acquisition) but positive EPV (0.34) and owner-earnings (0.65) MoS confirms real earnings power. With 17.5% gross margin and 5% net margin, DFH is significantly thinner than DHI/LEN (~22-25% gross) and LGIH (~25%), reflecting its land-lite/lot-option model which trades margin for capital efficiency (ROIC 11.9%). Revenue declined -2.9% YoY vs the broader housing recovery — a relative underperformance that explains the 26% 52-week position. DFH is the cheapest homebuilder on a normalized earnings basis, but the discount reflects its smaller scale, geographic concentration (Southeast/Mid-Atlantic), and below-average margin profile.
DFH.rationale: DFH is a smaller US homebuilder competing with D.R. Horton (DHI), Lennar (LEN), PulteGroup (PHM), Taylor Morrison (TMHC), LGI Homes (LGIH), and M/I Homes (MHO) in entry-level to move-up housing. Its 'asset-light' lot-option model differentiates it operationally but not competitively — end-market, buyer demographics, and capital cycle are identical to the peer group.

FDJU.PA.relative_comps: FDJU.PA (Française des Jeux — French national lottery operator) at 7.4x P/FCF with 32.9% gross margin and 16.7% ROIC is one of the more attractive gambling names on a risk-adjusted basis: the lottery concession provides quasi-utility revenue stability that neither DKNG nor online sportsbook peers can match. Revenue growth of +20% YoY and +14.3% 3Y CAGR is impressive, driven by digital lottery expansion. The EPS decline (-56% YoY) is likely a non-recurring item. At just 5% of its 52-week range FDJ is near a 52-week low despite strong revenue growth — this looks like an overreaction to margin or one-time pressure rather than a structural change, making it cheap vs peers on a quality-adjusted basis.
FDJU.PA.rationale: FDJU.PA is Française des Jeux (FDJ), France's state-licensed lottery and sports-betting operator. True peers are Flutter Entertainment (FLUT.L), Entain (ENT.L), DraftKings (DKNG), International Game Technology (IGT), and Casinos de Paris parent. FDJ is structurally lower-risk than online sportsbooks (regulated concession, lottery monopoly) — the correct comparables are regulated gaming concessionaires and national lottery operators globally.

FR.PA.relative_comps: FR.PA (Forvia SE, formerly Faurecia — Tier-1 auto seating/interior/exhaust/hydrogen) at 5.4x P/FCF appears cheap but the DCF MoS is -0.16 and multiple other methods are negative or inconclusive, suggesting the low multiple reflects genuine stress rather than mispricing. Gross margin (20.2%) and net margin (1%) are typical for Tier-1 auto suppliers, as is the low ROIC (3.8%). Revenue fell -2.7% YoY against a backdrop of EV transition uncertainty and OEM production cuts. Relative to APTV (Aptiv, ~14x, better margins) and LEA (Lear, ~8x, 5% net margin) FR.PA is the cheapest but also lowest quality — the Hella acquisition added debt and integration risk. The stock at 45% of 52-week range has limited recovery vs peers.
FR.PA.rationale: FR.PA is Forvia SE (formerly Faurecia), a major French Tier-1 automotive supplier (seating, interior systems, exhaust, hydrogen storage) that merged with Hella. True peers are Aptiv (APTV), Lear Corporation (LEA), BorgWarner (BWA), Tenneco (TEN.PA), Magna International (MGA), and Gestamp (GEST.MC). These share OEM customer concentration, cyclical exposure to light-vehicle production, and capital-intensive manufacturing.

FRVIA.PA.relative_comps: FRVIA.PA appears to be Forvia SE under its updated ticker (replacing FR.PA post-rename), sharing the same Tier-1 auto supplier economics but with worse near-term data: -20.9% revenue decline YoY, -9.8% net margin, and p_fcf of only 0.8x — an extreme distress valuation. The EPV MoS (0.63) and DCF MoS (0.78) suggest substantial margin of safety if the business normalizes, but the negative net margin and Altman-Z of 1.88 (distress zone) indicate genuine solvency risk. If this is the same entity as FR.PA the data divergence may reflect different reporting periods or a data feed artifact; if a distinct company it is the most distressed auto-parts name in the universe and cheap only with a high recovery assumption. Given the uncertainty, the verdict leans cheap but high-risk.
FRVIA.PA.rationale: FRVIA.PA is listed as an Auto-Parts company on Euronext Paris, closely related to or identical with Forvia SE (FR.PA). If distinct, it may be a subsidiary, tracking share, or a data-feed duplicate of the Forvia/Faurecia entity. The peer set is the same as FR.PA: Aptiv, Lear, BorgWarner, Magna, Gestamp, and Valeo (VAL.PA). NOTE: identity of FRVIA.PA vs FR.PA is uncertain — both carry near-identical price levels (~€11-12) and the same industry classification, suggesting possible ticker migration or data artifact; downstream users should verify the distinct legal entity before acting.

GEF.relative_comps: GEF (Greif — industrial packaging: steel/plastic drums, IBCs) at 0x reported P/FCF (FCF distorted by working capital) but graham_mos=0.82 and owner_earnings_mos=0.62 confirm strong normalized cash generation. Gross margin (22.2%) and ROIC (7.5%) are modest, consistent with commodity-cyclical industrial containers, but EPS surged +223% YoY (likely from a non-recurring gain or divestiture). Near 89% of its 52-week range — the stock has already moved — but still screens cheap on multiple method MoS. Relative to BALL (beverage cans, thinner margins at 14.9%, higher FCF multiple at 21.6x) and SLGN (~15x, consumer-facing packaging), GEF's industrial/B2B focus earns a lower multiple but the current price does not fully reflect normalized earnings power.
GEF.rationale: GEF (Greif Inc.) manufactures industrial packaging — steel and plastic drums, intermediate bulk containers (IBCs), and specialty containers for chemicals, oils, and food — competing with Mauser Packaging (private), Schutz (private), BWAY/Stone Canyon (private), and to a lesser degree BALL/CCK/SLGN in adjacent packaging categories. The industrial/B2B channel distinguishes GEF from consumer-facing packaging peers.

GEST.MC.relative_comps: GEST.MC (Gestamp Automoción — Spanish body-in-white metal stamping and welding) at 4.7x P/FCF is cheap in absolute terms, but the DCF MoS (-0.16) and EPV MoS (-0.17) are slightly negative, suggesting the low multiple may reflect fair value rather than undervaluation given slim margins. Gross margin (14.9%) and net margin (1.3%) are in line with other Tier-1/Tier-2 auto body suppliers; ROIC at 6.1% is thin. Revenue fell -5.4% YoY and the Altman-Z of 1.21 sits in the distress zone. The stock is at 11% of its 52-week range — severely depressed — but the fundamental picture (declining revenue, weak margins, distress-level Z-score) justifies the discount relative to Forvia (FR.PA) and Aptiv which carry better balance sheets and higher-value components.
GEST.MC.rationale: GEST.MC is Gestamp Automoción, a Spanish automotive body-structure and chassis-component manufacturer (hot stamping, cold stamping, roll forming) listed on Bolsa de Madrid. Its true peers are other Tier-1/Tier-2 structural auto suppliers: Forvia (FR.PA/FRVIA.PA), Magna (MGA), Tower International (private post-merger), Kirchhoff Automotive (private), and Martinrea (MRE.TO). The end-market is pure OEM capex/production cycle with no consumer brand.

GIII.relative_comps: GIII (G-III Apparel — wholesale apparel manufacturer with Donna Karan/DKNY/Karl Lagerfeld/Guess licenses) at 5.7x P/FCF with 38.4% gross margin and 8.8% ROIC is statistically cheap (DCF MoS 0.72, EPV MoS 0.26, acquirers_multiple hit). Revenue fell -7% YoY and EPS fell 64% — signaling either license-renewal headwinds or inventory normalization. At 83% of its 52-week range the market has recovered somewhat but the underlying earnings quality is weak (2.3% net margin). Relative to PVH (~10x, higher quality) and HBI (~6x, distressed) GIII screens cheap but its dependence on licensed brands (no owned global brand of scale) caps the quality premium it can earn vs a PVH or RL.
GIII.rationale: GIII Apparel is a US wholesale apparel manufacturer and licensor holding DKNY, Donna Karan, Karl Lagerfeld, Guess (license), and Karl Lagerfeld Paris — competing with PVH Corp (Calvin Klein/Tommy), Hanesbrands (HBI), Oxford Industries (Tommy Bahama/Lilly Pulitzer), Steve Madden (SHOO), and Capri Holdings (CPRI — Versace/Michael Kors/Jimmy Choo). The business model is brand licensing + wholesale distribution to department stores and specialty retail, making license-dependency and department-store exposure the key risk differentiators.

HRB.relative_comps: HRB (H&R Block — assisted and DIY tax preparation) is the cleanest value in its group: 8.5x P/FCF, 44.5% gross margin, 16.1% net margin, and 25.1% ROIC — returning capital at rates that rival much more expensive software businesses. The DCF MoS (0.65), owner-earnings MoS (0.64), and iv15 MoS (0.32) all confirm undervaluation. Revenue growth is slow (+4.2% YoY, +2.8% 3Y CAGR) — consistent with the secular shift to DIY tax software (INTU TurboTax) — but HRB has held share and expanded its digital offering. At 31% of its 52-week range HRB has pulled back despite strong profitability, making it the clearest relative mispricing in the group — cheap vs INTU (40x+), cheap vs the tax-services market it effectively duopolizes with INTU.
HRB.rationale: HRB competes in tax preparation services — directly against Intuit's TurboTax (INTU) in DIY software and against Jackson Hewitt/Liberty Tax in assisted preparation. HRB is unique as the largest assisted-prep franchise with 10,000+ offices plus a growing digital offering. True peers on economics are INTU (software-led, higher multiple) and the franchise-tax-services operators. The high ROIC/margin profile is more software-like than retail-like.

M.relative_comps: M (Macy's — US department store) at 6.8x P/FCF and an EV/GP methodology hit looks statistically cheap, but EPV MoS (-0.79) and DCF MoS (-0.11) are negative, and the EPV deeply negative signals the business does not earn its cost of capital on a sustainable earnings basis. Revenue declining -1.7% YoY and -3.9% 3Y CAGR against a backdrop of secular department-store traffic loss and ongoing real-estate monetization makes this a value-trap candidate. At 99% of its 52-week range the stock has already rallied sharply — possibly on the luxury Bloomingdale's/Blue Mercury optionality or real-estate NAV narrative. Relative to DDS (Dillard's, ~6x, but with superior real-estate ownership and buybacks) and JWN (Nordstrom, private-going), Macy's lacks Dillard's financial discipline and Nordstrom's brand equity, making the current premium to intrinsic value unjustified on normalized earnings.
M.rationale: M (Macy's) is a US full-line department store operator — its direct peers are Kohl's (KSS), Nordstrom (JWN), Dillard's (DDS), and Saks Fifth Avenue (private/HBC). The department-store model shares structurally declining traffic, owned/leased real-estate heavy balance sheets, and private-label margin dependency. The secondary narrative around Bloomingdale's and real-estate value makes M more complex than a pure-retail comp.

OPM.PA.relative_comps: OPmobility (OPM.PA) is an auto-parts supplier focused on bumpers, body panels, and hydrogen storage systems — direct peers are Forvia (FRVIA.PA) and Faurecia legacy (FR.PA) in the same Euronext universe, plus global Tier-1s Lear (LEA), Aptiv (APTV), Autoliv (ALV.DE), Magna (MGA), Standard Motor (SMP), and BorgWarner (BWA). OPM.PA trades at p_fcf ~4.0 with modest MoS scores (dcf 0.15, epv 0.17, owner_earnings 0.37), placing it broadly in-line to slightly cheap versus the depressed Tier-1 cohort — FRVIA.PA screens cheaper (p_fcf ~0.8, dcf MoS 0.78) and FR.PA is also distressed (negative dcf MoS). Revenue is flat to slightly down (-2.6% YoY) reflecting weak European auto production volumes; gross margin is very thin (9.4%) and net margin at 1.8% is typical for commoditised Tier-1 body parts. ROIC at 6% is below cost-of-capital for most of the peer group. Momentum is soft: price sits ~41% off the 52-week high with no clear catalyst, consistent with the broader European auto-parts de-rating. The cheapness versus intrinsic value is real but value-trap risk is high given structural headwinds (ICE-to-EV transition, OEM volume cuts, hydrogen timeline uncertainty).
OPM.PA.rationale: OPmobility competes directly with Forvia/Faurecia, Plastic Omnium legacy European peers, and global Tier-1 auto-parts suppliers on body exteriors and clean-energy modules. The screener-listed industry Auto-Parts is correct and the peer set is straightforwardly identifiable. Cheapness is genuine but cyclically/structurally compressed.

PAH3.DE.relative_comps: PAH3.DE is Porsche Automobil Holding SE — a pure holding company whose sole economic substance is a ~31% voting stake in Volkswagen AG and an indirect stake in Porsche AG. Its financials (revenue=0, gross_margin=1.0, net_margin=0.966) are holding-company artefacts: virtually all 'income' is equity-method earnings from VW. True peers are not OEM manufacturers but rather automotive holding/family entities: VW ordinary shares (VOW3.DE) itself, BMW (BMW.DE), Mercedes (MBG.DE), Stellantis (STLA), and the ADR POAHY.
