PAH3.DE trades at a persistent, structural discount to NAV (its VW stake value) because of the holding-company discount, the Dieselgate litigation overhang, and governance premium demanded by outside shareholders. p_fcf of 5.6 and strong graham/dcf MoS scores (0.77/0.71) reflect this NAV discount — the stock looks cheap on most metrics but that gap has persisted for years and is not a simple operational mispricing. Revenue CAGR is -19.6% (declining VW earnings) and ROIC is negative (-2.4%), confirming this is structurally a financial holding. Momentum is extremely weak: trading within 1.3% of its 52-week low. The 'cheapness' is structural/governance discount, not an earnings catalyst.
PAH3.DE.rationale: PAH3.DE is not an operating automaker but a holding company whose value equals its VW/Porsche AG stakes minus a persistent governance/NAV discount. Peers are the underlying OEM equities and comparable European family holding structures. The valuation discount is real but has been structurally persistent and is not an operational mispricing.

PDD.relative_comps: PDD Holdings (Pinduoduo / Temu) is a Chinese-origin e-commerce marketplace with a unique social/group-buying model domestically and an aggressive international expansion via Temu — peers are BABA and JD.com in China and global marketplace comps SE (Sea/Shopee), MELI (MercadoLibre), Coupang (CPNG), and Amazon. PDD screens compellingly cheap: p_fcf ~7.7, dcf MoS 0.66, graham MoS 0.83, iv15 MoS 0.80, with 56% gross margins and 22.7% net margins that are best-in-class for Chinese e-commerce. Revenue 3Y CAGR is 49% — dramatically ahead of any comparable at scale. However, proximity_52wk is only 0.07, implying the stock trades near 52-week lows despite strong fundamentals, reflecting geopolitical risk (US regulatory scrutiny of Temu, China ADR discount), slowing Pinduoduo domestic growth, and competitive pressure from Douyin/ByteDance commerce. The gap between quality/growth and valuation is among the largest in this universe — the cheapness appears real and mispricing-driven rather than quality-justified.
PDD.rationale: PDD is a high-quality, high-growth Chinese e-commerce platform (Pinduoduo + Temu). Its true peers are BABA, JD, and global marketplace operators. The screener industry tag 'Department Stores' understates the business model — it is a marketplace/platform, not a retailer. The cheapness vs intrinsic value and vs BABA/JD on margins and growth is real; the discount is geopolitical/sentiment, not structural quality weakness.

RL.relative_comps: Ralph Lauren (RL) is a premium American lifestyle brand — peers are PVH (Calvin Klein/Tommy Hilfiger), Tapestry (TPR, Coach/Kate Spade), Capri Holdings (CPRI, Michael Kors/Versace), and European luxury players LVMH, Kering (KER.PA), and Richemont (CFR.SW) on the aspirational end. RL has 69.9% gross margins and 11.6% net margins that sit comfortably above mid-market apparel peers (PVH ~40% gross, TPR ~70%) but well below pure luxury (LVMH ~65% gross with higher absolute margins). p_fcf of 34.3 is rich by value-screen standards and dcf MoS is deeply negative (-0.74), confirming the market is pricing in continued brand premiumisation — revenue grew 14.6% YoY, which is strong for the category, and the stock is near 52-week highs (proximity 0.93). RL trades rich vs mid-market peers on p_fcf but at a justified discount to true luxury; given the premium-to-luxury trading range RL has been executing into, the current valuation looks in-line rather than a clear mispricing in either direction.
RL.rationale: Ralph Lauren is a premium lifestyle apparel brand operating above mid-market (PVH/TPR) but below true luxury (LVMH/Kering). The peer set spans both bands. At p_fcf ~34 and near 52-week highs, RL is priced for continued brand elevation and margin expansion — expensive relative to mid-market peers but arguably in-line with its aspirational positioning. No obvious mispricing.

RVLV.relative_comps: Revolve Group (RVLV) is a US online fashion retailer targeting millennial/Gen-Z premium consumers — closest peers are ASOS (ASOS.L), Zalando (ZAL.DE), Farfetch (FTCH, now private/bankrupt), and domestically ANF and similar digitally-native apparel retailers. RVLV has 53.5% gross margins (premium to ZAL.DE's 37.4% and well above ASOS's ~44%), modest net margin (5%), and is recovering: revenue +8.5% YoY after prior contraction, EPS +25%. p_fcf of 35.7 is elevated and all intrinsic-value MoS scores are deeply negative or -1, meaning the model finds no margin of safety at current prices — it is trading on momentum/growth recovery expectations. Versus ZAL.DE (p_fcf 5.9, dcf MoS 0.41) RVLV looks significantly rich on an absolute FCF yield basis, though RVLV's US market and higher gross margins warrant some premium. Proximity to 52-week high is 0.45 — mid-range. The valuation is stretched relative to the peer set and the growth recovery does not yet justify the FCF multiple premium.
RVLV.rationale: Revolve competes with ASOS, Zalando, and other online-native fashion platforms. Its higher gross margin versus European peers is real but the p_fcf premium (~6x Zalando) is hard to justify on growth differentials alone. Rich vs the peer set with limited MoS across all methodologies.

SK.PA.relative_comps: SK.PA is Seb S.A., the French small-appliances group (Tefal, Rowenta, Krups, Moulinex, WMF) — a mid-cap consumer durables manufacturer with no clean listed pure-play peer; the closest are Philips (PHG.AS, overlapping in kitchen/personal care), private BSH (Bosch/Siemens home appliances), and to a lesser extent nVent or other appliance OEMs. Within the screener universe no direct peer is present. Seb has 40% gross margins and very thin net margins (3%) reflecting cost pressure and currency headwinds; revenue is essentially flat (-1.2% YoY). p_fcf of 105 is extremely high and likely reflects a FCF compression year (capex cycle or working capital) rather than structural overvaluation — the dcf MoS (0.32), epv MoS (0.26), and owner_earnings MoS (0.56) all show meaningful intrinsic-value upside despite the high p_fcf, which suggests FCF is temporarily depressed. The stock trades ~12% off its 52-week high with weak momentum. On a normalised FCF basis Seb would appear cheap vs peers, but the p_fcf multiple as reported is misleading.
SK.PA.rationale: Seb S.A. is Europe's dominant small-appliance group with limited direct listed peers. The extremely high reported p_fcf is a FCF-trough artefact; normalised MoS scores across dcf/epv/owner_earnings are positive and meaningful. The stock is mis-categorised relative to its complexity — it is genuinely cheap on normalised earnings but the FCF figure is noisy.

TUI1.DE.relative_comps: TUI AG (TUI1.DE) is Europe's largest vertically-integrated tour operator (hotels, airlines, cruise ships, retail travel shops) — a fundamentally different business model than pure OTA peers like Booking (BKNG) or Expedia (EXPE); closer comps are Thomas Cook legacy peers, Club Med (CVCB.PA, now private), Norwegian Cruise (NCLH), and Saga (SAGA.L). Within the universe BKNG is the nearest listed comp though it is asset-light vs TUI's capital-heavy model. TUI screens very cheap: p_fcf ~3.9, dcf MoS 0.81, owner_earnings MoS 0.74 — among the cheapest in the entire universe by MoS. However, net margin is only 2.6%, ROIC is near zero (0.36%), and Altman Z-score of 1.37 signals financial distress risk from the massive debt load taken during COVID recapitalisation. Revenue grew 4.4% YoY and EPS +16%. The cheapness is real but substantially explained by balance-sheet risk and thin margins — BKNG by contrast earns 20% net margins and 33% ROIC at p_fcf of 16. TUI is a deep-value, high-risk name; the valuation gap vs BKNG is justified by quality differences.
TUI1.DE.rationale: TUI is a capital-heavy vertically-integrated travel operator; its peers are other tour operators and cruise/hospitality businesses, not asset-light OTAs. The extreme cheapness on MoS metrics is real but offset by near-distress leverage and structurally low ROIC. The valuation discount vs BKNG is warranted but TUI cheap within its own risk tier.

WEN.relative_comps: Wendy's (WEN) is a US quick-service burger chain, competing directly with McDonald's (MCD), Burger King/Tim Hortons/Popeyes (QSR), Jack in the Box (JACK), and broadly with Yum! Brands (YUM) and Domino's (DPZ). WEN screens cheap on an owner_earnings basis (MoS 0.73) and moderately cheap on graham/dcf, with p_fcf of 6.2. However, revenue is slightly down (-3.1% YoY) and EPS fell 10.5% — the franchise-heavy QSR model normally provides stable cash flows but same-store sales softness and competitive pressure from McDonald's value campaigns is weighing on results. Gross margin (26.9%) is lower than MCD (~57%) or CMG (~26%), but net margin (7.6%) is consistent with the levered franchise model. Altman Z of 1.05 reflects significant leverage (franchisors typically carry debt). Relative to MCD (p_fcf historically 20-25x) and QSR (15-20x), WEN at ~6x FCF looks cheap, but WEN trades at a structural discount to MCD/QSR due to lower brand strength, slower unit growth, and weaker pricing power. The cheapness is partly justified but also partially a real discount that could close with improved value messaging and technology investment.
WEN.rationale: Wendy's is a pure-play QSR burger brand competing directly with McDonald's and Burger King. At p_fcf ~6x it screens cheap vs the QSR peer group (MCD typically 20-25x, QSR 15-20x). The discount partly reflects weaker brand momentum and leveraged balance sheet but also appears to include a real mispricing component if same-store sales recover.

ZAL.DE.relative_comps: Zalando (ZAL.DE) is Europe's largest online fashion and lifestyle platform, with a marketplace-plus-owned-inventory model — closest listed peers are ASOS (ASOS.L) and Revolve (RVLV) in pure online fashion, and vertically-integrated fast-fashion giants Inditex (ITX.MC) and H&M (HMB.ST) as end-market competitors for the European consumer's wallet. Zalando screens modestly cheap: p_fcf ~5.9, dcf MoS 0.41, graham MoS 0.46 — positive but not exceptional. Revenue grew a strong 16.8% YoY (acceleration from prior stagnation), gross margin is 37.4%, but net margin is thin at 1.7% and ROIC is only 4.3%, reflecting the asset-heavy platform build-out. Versus RVLV (p_fcf 35.7) ZAL looks dramatically cheaper on FCF yield, though ZAL's margins are lower. Versus ASOS (deeply loss-making) ZAL looks more stable. Momentum is improving: stock sits at 60% of 52-week high range. The cheapness vs RVLV is genuine — ZAL's scale advantage and European platform moat justify a narrower discount than currently implied. In-line to slightly cheap within the online-fashion peer group.
ZAL.DE.rationale: Zalando is the dominant European online fashion platform; its peers are ASOS, Revolve, and the online channels of Inditex/H&M. At p_fcf ~6 with 17% revenue growth, ZAL is significantly cheaper than US online-fashion comps on FCF multiples while carrying comparable or superior scale. The discount appears to reflect European platform re-rating risk and thin near-term margins rather than structural quality inferiority.

3969.HK.relative_comps: 3969.HK is China Railway Signal & Communication (CRSC), a state-backed railway signaling and equipment supplier. Its closest peers are CRRC Corporation (CRRC.HK, rolling stock), Zhuzhou CRRC Times Electric (3898.HK, traction systems), Wabtec (WAB, global rail tech), Alstom (ALSTM.PA, rail systems), and Siemens Mobility (within SIE.DE). On valuation, p_fcf=0 (no positive FCF reported) and EPV MoS of 81% looks optically cheap, but all other methods fail; ROIC_avg of 1.38 appears data-artefact-high and is suspect for a state-owned utility-like entity. The stock sits at just 6% of its 52-week range, suggesting deep price distress; revenue growth is modest at 6.4% YoY with a negative 3-year CAGR, indicating structural slow-down. Vs. WAB and Alstom the valuation looks cheap on a book basis, but the lack of free cash flow and state-owned-enterprise discount justify the discount — mispricing is partially real but the governance/FCF gap is a legitimate peer-discount driver.
3969.HK.rationale: CRSC is a Chinese state-owned railway signaling and equipment OEM; true peers are global rail technology suppliers (Wabtec, Alstom, Siemens Mobility) and Chinese rail SOEs (CRRC). Non-US .HK suffix confirmed from industry=Railroads + Chinese state-enterprise characteristics.

ATKR.relative_comps: Atkore Inc is a US manufacturer of electrical raceway products (steel/PVC conduit, cable trays) and mechanical products. True peers are nVent Electric (NVT), Eaton (ETN, electrical segment), Rexnord/Zurn (now merged), Legrand (LR.PA), and ABB (cable management). On valuation, p_fcf=9.0x is low for the sector, DCF MoS 72% and owner-earnings MoS 68% are strong, but revenue is declining -11% YoY and -10% 3-year CAGR reflecting a normalization from pandemic-era construction surge. Gross margin 22% and net margin near zero (-0.5%) flag margin compression; ROIC of 27.7% is historically strong but trending down. Vs. NVT (trades ~25x FCF) and ETN (~30x FCF), Atkore screens cheap — the valuation gap is partially justified by cyclical headwinds but the long-run ROIC argues for mean-reversion upside.
ATKR.rationale: Atkore manufactures electrical conduit, cable management, and mechanical products for construction and industrial end-markets; true peers are nVent, Eaton electrical segment, Legrand, and ABB cable management.

AXON.relative_comps: Axon Enterprise is the dominant provider of conducted energy weapons (TASER), body cameras, cloud evidence management (Axon Evidence), and AI-powered public safety software. It has no direct pure-play public peer; closest are OSIsoft/OSIS (now part of AVEVA/Schneider), Motorola Solutions (MSI, which competes in public safety comms/video), Leonardo DRS (DRS, defense electronics), and government IT services firms (LDOS, CACI, SAIC) for the software revenue base. On valuation, p_fcf=619x and all DCF methods fail — this is a high-growth SaaS/hardware compounder priced for a long runway. Revenue growth of 33% YoY with 27% 3-year CAGR is exceptional; gross margin 60% is best-in-class for the peer set. The stock trades at 23% of 52-week high, which is a pullback from peak but still expensive vs. any traditional industrial peer. The premium vs. MSI (trades ~35x FCF) is steep but partially justified by the software attach rate and switching-cost moat; however, at 619x FCF there is minimal margin of safety on any value metric.
AXON.rationale: Axon Enterprise's core business is law-enforcement hardware (TASER, body cameras) plus cloud evidence and AI software; closest public comps are Motorola Solutions (MSI) in public safety tech, and government IT firms for the software/analytics layer.

BAH.relative_comps: Booz Allen Hamilton is a US government IT and management consulting firm, heavily concentrated in defense/intelligence/civilian-agency work. True peers are SAIC, CACI International, Leidos (LDOS), ManTech (acquired by Carlyle), and Accenture Federal Services (within ACN). On valuation, p_fcf=8.1x is attractive vs. LDOS (~16x) and SAIC (~14x), and graham/owner-earnings MoS of 61-77% confirm the discount. Revenue is down -6% YoY and modest +6.6% 3-year CAGR, reflecting post-DOGE federal budget uncertainty and contract re-competition; EPS -5% YoY. The stock sits at 5% of its 52-week range (near the bottom), which is a technical negative but accentuates the value case. Vs. government IT peers, BAH screens genuinely cheap — the gap partly reflects near-term budget risk but not the sticky contract backlog and cleared workforce moat.
BAH.rationale: Booz Allen Hamilton is a US federal IT and management consulting firm; true peers are SAIC, CACI, Leidos, and Accenture Federal Services — all competing for the same DoD/intelligence agency contract awards.

DUE.DE.relative_comps: Duerr AG is a German industrial machinery group specializing in paint-finishing and assembly systems for the automotive industry, plus woodworking/clean technology equipment. True peers are Eisenmann (private), Geek+ (private), KION Group (KION.DE, intralogistics), and niche automation OEMs such as Grob-Werke (private) and Schuler (part of Andritz). On valuation, p_fcf=4.3x is very low, DCF MoS 64% and graham MoS 84% signal strong undervaluation; EPS grew +107% YoY (likely on one-time basis) while revenue dipped -2.9%. Gross margin 22% and net margin 4.9% are typical for capital-goods OEMs; ROIC 5% is modest. The stock sits at 8% of its 52-week range (near lows). Vs. KION (p_fcf ~11x) and peer machinery OEMs, Duerr is cheap — the discount reflects automotive-sector cyclicality and EV-transition uncertainty (auto OEM capex cuts), which is a real structural headwind but the order-book visibility and service recurring revenues reduce downside.
DUE.DE.rationale: Duerr AG is a German industrial machinery OEM focused on automotive paint/assembly automation and clean technology; true peers are KION Group, Schuler, and other European capital goods OEMs serving automotive and industrial end-markets.

FGR.PA.relative_comps: Eiffage SA is one of France's three major construction and concessions groups (alongside Vinci and Bouygues), with significant toll-road and infrastructure concession assets. True peers are Vinci (DG.PA / VCISF), ACS Group (ACS.MC), Bouygues (EN.PA), Hochtief (HOCHTIEF.DE, owned by ACS), and Balfour Beatty (BBY.L). On valuation, p_fcf=3.97x is very low, DCF MoS 68% and owner-earnings MoS 62% are compelling; revenue growth of 8.8% YoY and 7.8% 3-year CAGR is solid for a large contractor. Gross margin 12.9% and net margin 3.9% are typical for the E&C model; ROIC 6.3% reflects the capital intensity of concessions. Vs. Vinci (trades ~12x FCF, higher-quality concession book) and ACS, Eiffage is cheap on FCF yield but the discount is partially explained by lower concession mix and higher construction exposure. The gap appears larger than justified — Eiffage's APRR motorway concession (one of France's best) is an embedded value the market underprices.
FGR.PA.rationale: Eiffage is a French construction and infrastructure-concession group; true peers are Vinci (DG.PA), ACS Group (ACS.MC), Bouygues (EN.PA), and Hochtief (HOCHTIEF.DE) — all large European contractors with concession portfolios.

GBX.relative_comps: Greenbrier Companies is a US manufacturer of freight railcars (tank cars, intermodal, boxcars) and operates a railcar leasing and management services segment. True peers are Trinity Industries (TRN), American Railcar Industries (ARII, now part of ITE Management), GATX Corporation (GATX, rail leasing), and indirectly Wabtec (WAB, rail components/systems). On valuation, p_fcf=0 (negative/zero FCF), graham MoS 70% is the only live method; revenue is down -8.7% YoY but EPS grew +28% suggesting margin expansion. Gross margin 18.6% and net margin 6.3% are acceptable for a capital-intensive OEM/leasor; ROIC 1.8% is low. Vs. TRN (p_fcf ~10x, better ROIC, more leasing) and GATX (steady lease book), GBX screens modestly cheap on book/earnings but the low ROIC and cyclical orderbook risk limit the upside case — the discount is partially justified.
GBX.rationale: Greenbrier Companies manufactures freight railcars and operates a railcar leasing/services business; true peers are Trinity Industries (TRN), GATX Corporation, and indirectly Wabtec for components — all competing in North American freight railcar markets.

GE.relative_comps: GE Aerospace (formerly General Electric, post-spin) is a pure-play commercial and defense jet engine maker (LEAP, GE9X, GE90) with high-margin aftermarket services. True peers are RTX (Pratt & Whitney engines + Collins Aerospace), Safran (SAFGY, CFM joint venture partner with GE), Rolls-Royce (RR.L), MTU Aero Engines (MTU.DE), and Heico (HEI, aftermarket parts). On valuation, p_fcf=54x is rich, all DCF methods fail, and the stock sits near its 52-week high (92% proximity). Revenue grew +18.5% YoY and +16.3% 3-year CAGR; EPS +36%; gross margin 37% and net margin 19% are sector-leading. Vs. RTX (~25x FCF) and Safran (~20x FCF), GE trades at a meaningful premium, which is partially justified by its engine services earnings power and the commercial aviation upcycle, but the FCF multiple leaves little room for error.
GE.rationale: GE Aerospace is a jet engine manufacturer and high-margin MRO/aftermarket services provider; true peers are RTX (Pratt & Whitney), Safran (CFM JV partner), Rolls-Royce, MTU Aero Engines, and Heico for aftermarket.

GIC.relative_comps: Global Industrial Company (GIC) is a US B2B industrial supplies and MRO distributor selling via catalog/e-commerce to business customers. True peers are W.W. Grainger (GWW), MSC Industrial Direct (MSM), Fastenal (FAST), and DXPE Enterprises (DXPE). On valuation, p_fcf=18.1x is in-line with the distribution sector; DCF and EPV MoS are modestly negative to zero, suggesting fair value. Gross margin 35.5% is better than MSM (~42%) but below Grainger (~38%) — the margin profile is decent for catalog distribution. Revenue growth of 4.8% YoY and 5.8% 3-year CAGR is steady; ROIC of 21.7% is genuinely high and comparable to Grainger. The stock is at 71% of 52-week range, in-line trend. Vs. GWW (p_fcf ~25x, larger scale) and MSM (~14x, slower growth), GIC screens in-line to slightly rich given the smaller scale, though ROIC quality is real.
GIC.rationale: Global Industrial Company is a US B2B industrial MRO distributor; true peers are W.W. Grainger (GWW), MSC Industrial Direct (MSM), and Fastenal (FAST) — all competing for the same SMB and mid-market industrial supply buyers.

HEI.relative_comps: Heico Corporation is a US manufacturer of FAA-approved replacement parts (PMA parts) for commercial and defense aircraft, with a strong parts distribution arm. True peers are TransDigm Group (TDG, the closest comp — sole-source aerospace components at premium pricing), GE Aerospace (engine parts), RTX (Collins Aerospace MRO), and Spirit AeroSystems (SPR, airframe structures). On valuation, p_fcf=56x is very rich and all value methods fail; the stock trades at 84% of its 52-week high. Revenue grew +16.3% YoY and +26.6% 3-year CAGR; EPS +33.5%; gross margin 40% and net margin 15.4% are excellent. Vs. TDG (p_fcf ~25x, higher leverage but similar PMA moat), Heico trades at a steep premium — the gap partly reflects Heico's family-controlled capital allocation discipline and lower leverage, but 56x FCF is stretched even for a high-quality compounder.
HEI.rationale: Heico makes FAA-approved PMA parts (aftermarket aviation components) and distributes aerospace/defense parts; its closest true peer is TransDigm Group (TDG), both operating in the high-barrier sole-source aerospace aftermarket.

HUBG.relative_comps: Hub Group is a US intermodal transportation and logistics company, operating primarily as a non-asset-based intermodal broker/intermediary and a trucking/dray fleet. True peers are J.B. Hunt (JBHT, intermodal leader), Landstar System (LSTR, asset-light), C.H. Robinson (CHRW, freight brokerage), and Echo Global Logistics (ECHO). On valuation, p_fcf=18.6x with only minimal DCF MoS (1.8%) and negative EPV MoS — in-line to slightly elevated given the freight-cycle downturn. Revenue is down -6.1% YoY and -2.3% 3-year CAGR; gross margin of 85.4% looks anomalous and likely reflects how Hub reports net revenue (brokerage model). Net margin 2.6% and ROIC 8.2% are thin. The stock is at 54% of its 52-week range, recovering from lows. Vs. JBHT (p_fcf ~20x, owns more assets) and CHRW (~18x, larger network), Hub is in-line on FCF multiple but with inferior scale; the 3PL/intermodal recovery cycle could compress the discount.
HUBG.rationale: Hub Group is a US intermodal transportation and logistics intermediary; true peers are J.B. Hunt (JBHT), C.H. Robinson (CHRW), Landstar System (LSTR), and Echo Global Logistics — all competing in the US freight brokerage and intermodal markets.

IT.relative_comps: Gartner Inc (IT) is a research and advisory firm selling subscription-based technology research, data, and consulting to enterprise C-suite buyers. True peers in methodology/model are Forrester Research (FORR), Info Services Group (III), and more broadly MSCI (MSCI), S&P Global (SPGI), Verisk Analytics (VRSK), and Moody's (MCO) as high-multiple data/subscription businesses. On valuation, p_fcf=8.3x is attractively low for a subscription-research compounder; DCF MoS 63% and owner-earnings MoS 58% confirm genuine undervaluation; gross margin 67.7% and ROIC 21% are excellent. Revenue growth is modest at 3.7% YoY but steady; EPS declined -40% likely on a comp/non-recurring basis. The stock sits at only 4% of its 52-week range (near lows), a meaningful technical negative. Vs. SPGI (~30x FCF) and VRSK (~35x), Gartner is materially cheaper despite comparable subscription economics and pricing power — the gap appears to be a genuine mispricing driven by recent sentiment/multiple compression.
IT.rationale: Gartner is a technology research and advisory subscription business; true peers on economics are MSCI, S&P Global, Verisk Analytics, and Moody's — all high-margin, low-churn data/subscription franchises — and direct research competitors Forrester and IDC.

JUN3.DE.relative_comps: Jungheinrich AG is a German forklift and intralogistics systems manufacturer (forklifts, warehouse automation, WMS software), listed on Xetra (JUN3.DE). True peers are KION Group (KION.DE, parent of Linde Material Handling and Dematic), Crown Equipment (private), Toyota Industries TMHG division (private), and Hyster-Yale (HY). On valuation, p_fcf=6.2x is low; DCF MoS 62%, EPV MoS 64%, and owner-earnings MoS 73% all confirm undervaluation; the stock is at 5% of its 52-week range (near lows). Revenue growth is modest at +2% YoY and EPS declined -64% YoY (a sharp earnings dip, likely from restructuring charges or softening demand). Gross margin 32% and ROIC 5.8% are below KION's historical norms. Vs. KION (trades ~9x FCF on recovery expectations) and Hyster-Yale (~8x), Jungheinrich screens cheap — the EPS decline and near-term European warehouse-automation demand softness justify some discount but the FCF/EPV metrics suggest excess pessimism.
JUN3.DE.rationale: Jungheinrich manufactures forklifts and intralogistics/warehouse automation systems; true peers are KION Group (KION.DE), Crown Equipment, Toyota Industries material handling, and Hyster-Yale — all competing in the global forklift and warehouse automation market.

LIGHT.AS.relative_comps: LIGHT.AS is Signify NV (formerly Philips Lighting), the global leader in professional and consumer LED lighting, listed on Euronext Amsterdam. True peers are ams OSRAM (listed on Xetra), Acuity Brands (AYI), Zumtobel Group (ZUMTOB.VI), and Cree/Wolfspeed (Wolfspeed, now focused on SiC). On valuation, p_fcf=4.9x is very low; DCF MoS 65%, EPV MoS 46%, and owner-earnings MoS 65% confirm cheap valuation; the stock trades at 3% of its 52-week range (near multi-year lows). Revenue declined -6.2% YoY and -8.5% 3-year CAGR; EPS fell -21% — persistent top-line decline is the core problem. Gross margin 40% is decent; ROIC 6.9% is modest. Vs. AYI (trades ~18x FCF, growing) and ams OSRAM (structural loss-maker), Signify sits in between — genuinely cheap on FCF but the secular commoditization of LED and aggressive Chinese competition (NVC, Opple) is a real structural headwind that partially justifies the depressed multiple.
LIGHT.AS.rationale: Signify NV (LIGHT.AS) is the world's largest lighting company by revenue; true peers are Acuity Brands (AYI), ams OSRAM, Zumtobel Group, and Cree/Wolfspeed for LED technology — all competing in professional, architectural, and consumer lighting.

LZ.relative_comps: LegalZoom (LZ) is a US online legal services platform providing document preparation, registered agent, and compliance services to small businesses and consumers. True peers are Rocket Lawyer (private), Clerky (private), Northwest Registered Agent (private), and at the SMB-platform level, Intuit (INTU, TurboTax/QuickBooks ecosystem), Wolters Kluwer (WKL.AS, legal/compliance software), and ZenBusiness (private). On valuation, p_fcf=7.6x looks cheap; DCF MoS 36% but EPV/graham methods fail; ROIC is negative (-6.8%) — a key red flag, and EPS down 50% YoY. Revenue growth of 10.9% YoY is reasonable for a platform, but net margin is near zero (2%). The stock is near its 52-week low (12% of range). LZ lacks the switching-cost moat of Wolters Kluwer and the network effects of Intuit; its low-cost niche is being attacked by AI legal tools. Vs. private pure-play peers the valuation may look fair, but the negative ROIC and commoditization risk make the apparent cheapness a value trap.
LZ.rationale: LegalZoom is a US online legal document and SMB compliance platform; true peers in economics are Rocket Lawyer and ZenBusiness (both private), and Wolters Kluwer (WKL.AS) and Intuit (INTU) as adjacent legal/compliance software providers with stronger moats.

MMS.relative_comps: Maximus Inc (MMS) provides government-outsourced program management services (Medicaid enrollment, welfare-to-work, health insurance exchanges) primarily to US state and federal agencies. True peers are Conduent (CNDT, government BPO), ICF International (ICFI), SAIC, and Leidos (LDOS) for the federal side, and Accenture Federal Services (within ACN) for health IT. On valuation, p_fcf=8.6x; graham MoS 79% and convergence methodology selected confirm deep value; stock at 2% of 52-week range (near lows). Revenue growth only 2.4% YoY; EPS +10%; gross margin 23% and net margin 5.9% are typical for government services margins. ROIC 9.1% is decent. Vs. LDOS (~16x FCF) and ICFI (~18x FCF), Maximus is materially cheaper — the discount reflects DOGE federal spending cuts risk and Medicaid redetermination revenue cliffs, but the backlog-driven visibility partially insulates it. The gap looks excessive relative to the sticky contract nature of government BPO.
MMS.rationale: Maximus Inc provides outsourced government program administration services (Medicaid, welfare programs, health exchanges) to US federal and state agencies; true peers are ICF International (ICFI), Conduent (CNDT), Leidos (LDOS), and SAIC.

NSP.relative_comps: Insperity (NSP) is a US professional employer organization (PEO) that co-employs SMB workers, administering HR, payroll, benefits, and compliance on behalf of small businesses. True peers are TriNet Group (TNET, the closest public comp — also a PEO), ADP TotalSource (within ADP), Paychex (PAYX, broader HR/payroll), and Oasis (private, PEO). On valuation, p_fcf=0 (no positive FCF in the period), but DCF MoS 45% and owner-earnings MoS 25% suggest underlying earnings power; ROIC 16.5% is strong. Revenue growth 3.5% YoY and 4.7% 3-year CAGR is steady; EPS flat; net margin near zero (-0.1%) due to pass-through benefits cost structure (gross margin 13.2% is depressed by benefits pass-through, not the real margin). The stock is at 51% of its 52-week range, mid-range. Vs. TNET (p_fcf ~15x, higher margin, enterprise focus) and PAYX (~28x FCF), NSP screens cheap — the low stated margins are an accounting artifact of the PEO model, and the underlying HR services economics are more attractive than they appear.
NSP.rationale: Insperity is a US professional employer organization (PEO) co-employing SMB workers; the closest public comp is TriNet Group (TNET), with ADP TotalSource and Paychex as adjacent HR/payroll providers — all competing for SMB HR outsourcing spend.

PLX.PA.relative_comps: [PEER ANCHOR CORRECTED — Radar mis-mapped PLX.PA as 'PLX.PA is Pluxee NV (formerly Sodexo Benefits &…'] Pluxee (PLX.PA): anchor peer EDEN.PA now trades ~10.3x fwd P/E / ~4.1x EV/EBITDA (Oddo BHF / sell-side 2026e (de-rated post-Brazil PAT + Italy reform)), NOT the pre-shock 20-25x cited from memory in older dossiers. Pluxee (PLX.PA) and Edenred (EDEN.PA) are the employee-benefits/voucher near-duopoly and BOTH de-rated on the SAME regulatory shock — the Brazil PAT reform + Italy voucher cap (Kepler cut PLX EUR28->EUR18 and EDEN EUR40->EUR28 on the same event). Edenred now trades ~10x fwd P/E / ~4x EV/2026e EBITDA, NOT its pre-shock 20-25x. Pluxee at ~5x P/FCF vs a ~10x Edenred is a MODEST discount, largely justified by Pluxee's heavier Brazil concentration (guided ~50% Brazil revenue drop; Edenred took an 8-12% group EBITDA hit on the same reforms). The convergence is SECTOR-REGULATORY BETA on a shared, unresolved factor — NOT idiosyncratic single-name alpha — which is exactly what a correlation-stressed apex basket is built to discount.

Edenred now trades ~10x fwd P/E / ~4x EV/2026e EBITDA, NOT its pre-shock 20-25x. Pluxee at ~5x P/FCF vs a ~10x Edenred is a MODEST discount, largely justified by Pluxee's heavier Brazil concentration (guided ~50% Brazil revenue drop; Edenred took an 8-12% group EBITDA hit on the same reforms). The convergence is SECTOR-REGULATORY BETA on a shared, unresolved factor — NOT idiosyncratic single-name alpha — which is exactly what a correlation-stressed apex basket is built to discount.
