PRG.relative_comps: PROG Holdings (PRG) operates Progressive Leasing, a virtual rent-to-own platform embedded in retail point-of-sale for consumer durables (furniture, electronics, appliances) — i.e., a lease-to-own fintech/BNPL for credit-constrained consumers. True peers are Rent-A-Center / Upbound Group (RCII/UPBD), Aaron's Holdings (AAN, also under Upbound), and Conn's (CONN). On valuation, p_fcf=5.8x; EPV MoS 67% and owner-earnings MoS 85% are strong; stock at 100% of its 52-week range (at highs). Revenue flat (-2.2% YoY); EPS -21% YoY; gross margin 33% and net margin 6.1%, ROIC 12.6%. PRG sits at its 52-week high despite modest fundamentals. Vs. UPBD (~5x FCF, comparable lease-to-own economics), PRG is roughly in-line on price — but at the 52-week high with declining EPS, the momentum is already priced in. The EPV/owner-earnings MoS may reflect conservative fair-value anchoring rather than a real discount to the traded multiple of peers.
PRG.rationale: PROG Holdings operates a virtual rent-to-own/lease-to-own platform at retail POS; its closest peers are Upbound Group (UPBD/RCII) and Aaron's Holdings (AAN) — all serving credit-constrained consumers with lease-to-own financing for consumer durables.

TEP.PA.relative_comps: TEP.PA is Teleperformance SE, the world's largest business process outsourcing / customer experience management (CXM) company, with call centers and back-office operations globally. True peers are Concentrix (CNXC), TTEC Holdings (TTEC), Conduent (CNDT), Atento (ATTO), and Synnex/Concentrix. On valuation, p_fcf=2.4x is extraordinarily low; DCF MoS 82%, EPV MoS 67%, and owner-earnings MoS 81% — multiple methods converging on deep undervaluation; stock at 14% of 52-week range. Revenue roughly flat (-0.7% YoY) and EPS -3.6%; gross margin 11.8% and net margin 4.9% are typical BPO margins; ROIC 7.9% is modest. Vs. Concentrix (CNXC, trades ~5x FCF post-integration, similar BPO) and TTEC (~6x FCF), TEP is dramatically cheaper — the discount reflects the AI/automation-threat narrative (LLMs displacing call-center agents) and Majorel acquisition integration overhang, but at 2.4x FCF the market is pricing near-zero terminal value. The valuation appears to price in structural decline that is likely more gradual than feared.
TEP.PA.rationale: Teleperformance SE (TEP.PA) is the world's largest CXM/BPO company managing outsourced customer interactions globally; true peers are Concentrix (CNXC), TTEC Holdings, and Conduent — all competing in the global customer experience outsourcing market.

TKA.DE.relative_comps: ThyssenKrupp is a sprawling German conglomerate best anchored to European integrated steel and industrial-systems peers: voestalpine, Salzgitter, and Outokumpu on the steel side; Aperam for specialty/stainless; and its elevator/automotive-tech arms overlap with Schindler and Continental. On valuation, TKA.DE screens with p_fcf of 48.9x — deceptively high because FCF is near zero on deeply negative ROIC (−2%), not a growth premium; all DCF/EPV/Graham MoS signals are negative or null, confirming the multiple reflects distressed earnings, not moat pricing power. Growth is declining (rev CAGR −7.2%, YoY −6.3%), gross margin is a thin 11.3%, and net margin 1.4% — far below Aperam (~6-8%) and voestalpine (~3-4%) and dramatically worse than Outokumpu post-restructuring. Trend is mixed: trading near a 65% proximity to 52-wk high but SMA200 is only €9.73, suggesting a recent bounce off deeply oversold levels. The valuation gap vs peers is NOT a mispricing signal — it reflects ongoing structural losses, heavy pension liabilities, and the unresolved steel-demerger execution risk; this is a deep-value/turnaround call, not a cheap compounder.
TKA.DE.rationale: ThyssenKrupp (TKA.DE) is a German integrated steel + industrial conglomerate; true peers are European steelmakers (voestalpine, Salzgitter, Outokumpu/Aperam for specialty steel) and diversified industrials. Its p_fcf multiple is inflated by near-zero FCF on negative ROIC — this is not cheapness, it is distress. Ranked poorer than peers on every quality metric.

TNET.relative_comps: TriNet is a US Professional Employer Organization (PEO) — it co-employs SMB workers, bundles payroll, benefits, and HR administration, and earns on spread between insurance premiums collected and claims paid. Its true peers are ADP (TotalSource PEO division), Paychex PEO, and Insperity (NSP), which is the closest pure-play listed comparable. On valuation, TNET trades at p_fcf of 7.8x vs NSP at roughly 12-15x, with robust multi-method MoS confirmation (DCF 72.5%, EPV 40.4%, OE 61.0%) — this is genuinely cheap relative to the PEO peer set. TNET's 30-35bps lower gross margin (17.4%) vs NSP (~25%) reflects a slightly more insurance-heavy mix, but net margin (3.1%) and ROIC (21.4%) compare favorably. Revenue is flat YoY (−0.9%) with modest 3Y CAGR, reflecting PEO industry maturation and SMB headcount softness — NSP faces the same headwind. Price is at 38% of 52-wk range and below SMA200, reflecting sector pessimism that is not matched by earnings quality; the valuation gap to NSP appears a real mispricing given superior ROIC.
TNET.rationale: TriNet (TNET) is a US PEO/HR outsourcing company; closest listed peer is Insperity (NSP), with ADP and Paychex as scale benchmarks. TNET's p_fcf of 7.8x and strong multi-method MoS spread (DCF 72.5%, EPV 40.4%) place it materially cheaper than NSP at current multiples despite comparable ROIC (21.4%) — the discount is driven by near-term earnings softness and PEO industry margin normalization, which appears cyclical rather than structural.

UPWK.relative_comps: Upwork is a two-sided online labor marketplace connecting businesses with freelancers; its closest listed comparable is Fiverr (FVRR), which serves the same end-market with a different positioning (fixed-price gigs vs. hourly/project). UPWK trades at p_fcf of 4.8x with a DCF MoS of 50% — cheaper than FVRR which has traded at 15-25x FCF on similar or worse growth. UPWK's gross margin of 77.8% is consistent with a marketplace take-rate model (superior to traditional staffing), but ROIC is effectively zero (−0.5%) because the business has been reinvesting heavily. Revenue growth has decelerated sharply (2.4% YoY vs. 8.4% CAGR), reflecting enterprise contract softness and macro headwinds on discretionary project spend. Trend is weak — trading at 6% of 52-wk high with price well below SMA200 ($14.90), signaling persistent momentum deterioration. The valuation discount to FVRR is partially justified by UPWK's higher enterprise exposure (more cyclical), but at p_fcf of 4.8x the market is pricing near-zero terminal growth, which is pessimistic given ~78% gross margins.
UPWK.rationale: Upwork (UPWK) is an online freelance labor marketplace; Fiverr (FVRR) is the closest listed peer, with Freelancer.com a distant second. UPWK trades at p_fcf ~4.8x vs. FVRR's structurally higher multiples, with 77.8% gross margins and DCF MoS of 50% — genuinely cheap versus the peer set, with the discount reflecting cyclical enterprise-spend softness rather than a structural platform deterioration.

VEI.OL.relative_comps: Veidekke ASA is a Nordic (Norwegian/Swedish) construction and civil-engineering contractor focused on building, civil works, and real-estate development — its true peer set is Scandinavian contractors: AF Gruppen, Skanska, NCC, and Peab. VEI.OL screens with p_fcf of 96x — extremely elevated — but the gross_margin = 1.0 is a data artifact (likely construction revenue net of subcontractors reported as 100% gross margin); the meaningful comparison is net margin (3.6%) which is normal for construction. ROIC of 20% is strong vs peers (Skanska ~12%, NCC ~10%), and EPS growth +23.6% YoY is above the peer group. All valuation MoS signals are null/negative (-1.0), meaning no method fires conviction on cheapness; only the EV/gross profit method triggered, which on a margin-compressed contractor is a loose signal. Trend is strong: 86% proximity to 52-wk high with SMA200 well below price. VEI.OL appears rich vs. peers on any FCF-based multiple (AF Gruppen typically trades 8-12x), with the premium somewhat justified by superior ROIC but not fully by current growth rates.
VEI.OL.rationale: Veidekke (VEI.OL) is a Norwegian construction contractor with operations across Scandinavia; peers are AF Gruppen, Skanska, NCC, and Peab. The p_fcf of 96x is extreme vs. peer group trading at 8-15x FCF; the premium reflects strong ROIC (20%) and near-52wk-high momentum but is difficult to justify fully on fundamentals vs. the listed Scandinavian construction peer set.

VRT.relative_comps: Vertiv designs and makes power/cooling infrastructure for data centers (UPS, thermal management, switchgear, PDUs) — it is a direct beneficiary of AI-driven data center build-out. True peers are Eaton, Emerson (DCSS segment), Schneider Electric, and ABB on the power/cooling side; nVent Electric for more niche enclosure/thermal overlap. VRT trades at p_fcf of 62.7x — premium vs. Eaton (~25x) and Schneider (~20x FCF), but VRT is growing revenue +27.7% YoY (3Y CAGR +21.6%) vs. Eaton at high-single-digit and Schneider at mid-single-digit. All MoS valuation methods are null (growth profile overwhelms value screens), indicating the market is pricing in continued hyper-growth. Gross margin (34.4%) is below Eaton/Schneider (~40-45%) reflecting more hardware-intensive mix, but the ROIC improvement trajectory (10.5% and rising) and net margin expansion (+13%) are positive. Price at 72% of 52-wk high and above SMA200 confirms strong momentum alignment with the AI infrastructure theme. The rich multiple vs. traditional electrical equipment peers is largely justified by superior revenue growth velocity, though re-rating risk is high if data center capex moderates.
VRT.rationale: Vertiv (VRT) manufactures data center power and cooling infrastructure; its true peers are Eaton, Schneider Electric, Emerson, and ABB — all significantly lower-growth incumbents. VRT's p_fcf of 62.7x is a steep premium to the peer group (20-25x), but the premium is largely justified by 28% revenue growth driven by AI data center demand; the risk is valuation compression if hyperscaler capex cycles down.

WKL.AS.relative_comps: Wolters Kluwer is a Dutch professional information and software company serving legal, tax, health, and financial compliance markets — its true peers are RELX, Thomson Reuters, and Verisk on the information-services side; SPGI for compliance/regulatory analytics. WKL.AS trades at p_fcf of 9.0x with DCF MoS 54.4%, graham MoS 65.1%, OE MoS 57.4% — materially cheaper than RELX (~20x FCF) and Thomson Reuters (~30x+ FCF), which trade at significant brand/moat premiums. WKL's gross margin of 73.5% and net margin of 21.4% are consistent with the peer group (RELX ~31% net, TRI ~20% net), and ROIC of 17% is competitive. Revenue growth is modest (3.5% YoY, 3.9% 3Y CAGR) — below RELX (~8%) and VRSK (~7%), explaining some of the discount. Trend is weak: trading at 3% of 52-wk high and well below SMA200 ($79.67), which is a significant momentum headwind. The valuation discount to RELX and TRI is partially justified by lower growth, but at 9x FCF the market appears to be pricing in margin compression or competitive erosion that is not yet evident in fundamentals — this looks like a real mispricing on a quality information-services compounder.
WKL.AS.rationale: Wolters Kluwer (WKL.AS) is a Dutch professional information and compliance software company; RELX and Thomson Reuters are the closest listed peers. WKL trades at p_fcf ~9x with multi-method MoS confirmation (DCF 54%, Graham 65%) vs. RELX at ~20x and TRI at 30x+ FCF — a material discount that is only partially explained by lower revenue growth, and appears to represent genuine relative cheapness for a high-quality information-services franchise.

WLDN.relative_comps: Willdan Group provides engineering, energy efficiency, and sustainability consulting services primarily to US municipalities, utilities, and federal clients — its closest listed peers are Tetra Tech (TTEK), NV5 Global, and ICF International, all of which serve government/infrastructure end-markets with similar capital-light consulting models. WLDN trades at p_fcf of 17.3x, but DCF MoS is deeply negative (−69.2%) and all other MoS signals are null or negative, indicating the screener sees the stock as overvalued on an intrinsic basis. Revenue growth is strong (+20.5% YoY, +16.7% 3Y CAGR) driven by infrastructure bill and energy-transition tailwinds, outpacing TTEK (~12%) and NV5 (~10%). However, gross margin (37.5%) and net margin (7.7%) are below TTEK (~25% net) and ICF (~8% net), and ROIC at 4.1% is well below peer group averages (TTEK ~15%, NV5 ~8%). The stock sits at 26% of 52-wk high and below SMA200 ($96.90), suggesting recent momentum deterioration despite the growth narrative. WLDN's growth premium is hard to sustain given sub-par ROIC vs. the peer group, and it screens as rich relative to value-creating comparable government consultants.
WLDN.rationale: Willdan Group (WLDN) is a US government/municipal engineering and energy consulting firm; Tetra Tech, NV5 Global, and ICF International are the closest listed peers. Despite strong revenue growth driven by infrastructure spending, WLDN's ROIC (4.1%) and margins significantly trail the peer group, and its DCF MoS is deeply negative, placing it rich vs. higher-quality comparables like TTEK that compound at superior returns on invested capital.

AFX.DE.relative_comps: AFX.DE is Carl Zeiss Meditec (German medical optics/surgical microscopes/ophthalmology equipment), NOT a CRO or generic medtech. True peers are ISRG (robotic surgery), SYK (surgical instruments), EW (cardiac), HOLX (minimally invasive), EMED.L (Natus/neuro), and German medtech SIEMENS HEALTHINEERS (SHL.DE) / Carl Zeiss Vision siblings. At p_fcf=13x with 53% gross margins and 9% ROIC, AFX.DE trades at a material discount to US medtech device comps (IHI/ISRG at 30-50x FCF) but in-line with Siemens Healthineers and Hologic. Revenue growth of +8% is solid for capital equipment, but net margin compression to 6% (vs 15%+ peers) from China headwinds and R&D drag make the gap partially justified — cheap vs global peers but not a screaming mispricing given margin pressure.
AFX.DE.rationale: Carl Zeiss Meditec (AFX.DE) is a German precision medical optics company competing in surgical microscopes, ophthalmology, and neurosurgery equipment; true peers are SHL.DE (Siemens Healthineers), ISRG, EW, HOLX, and Topcon/Nidek in ophthalmic capital equipment — not CROs or diversified medtech. Its current depressed valuation (p_fcf 13x vs US medtech 30-50x) reflects genuine margin compression from China softness and integration costs, not a permanent structural discount.

ALNY.relative_comps: Alnylam is the category leader in RNA interference (RNAi) therapeutics for rare diseases; closest peers are Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS, antisense oligonucleotides), Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR, RNAi), Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA, gene editing), BioMarin (BMRN, rare disease enzymes), and Ultragenyx (RARE, rare metabolic). At p_fcf=84x on just-turned-profitable status and 65% rev CAGR 3y, ALNY trades at a substantial premium to IONS (p_fcf ~15-20x) and BMRN (~25x), but the premium is largely justified by its proprietary GalNAc delivery platform moat, multiple approved drugs (inclisiran royalties, vutrisiran, patisiran), and a commercial inflection still in early innings. Gross margin of 82% with revenue just achieving profitability puts it in-line with BMRN/RARE at comparable commercialization stages; price at 8% of 52wk high signals recent selloff creating entry opportunity vs fundamentals.
ALNY.rationale: Alnylam competes in RNAi/RNA-targeted rare disease; true comps are IONS (antisense), ARWR (RNAi), NTLA (gene editing), BMRN and RARE (rare disease enzymes) — all gene/RNA modality platforms with similar 60-80% gross margins and late-stage commercial profiles. Premium to IONS is warranted by platform breadth and GalNAc moat; current 52wk-low proximity suggests temporary dislocation rather than structural overvaluation.

AXSM.relative_comps: Axsome Therapeutics is a commercial-stage CNS specialty pharma focused on treatment-resistant depression (AXS-05/Auvelity), narcolepsy, and migraine; true peers are Intra-Cellular Therapies (ITCI), ACADIA Pharmaceuticals (ACAD), Sage Therapeutics (SAGE), Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ), Praxis Precision Medicine (PRAX), and Supernus Pharmaceuticals (SUPN). With p_fcf=0 (FCF-negative but rapidly ramping Auvelity sales driving 66% revenue growth) and 93% gross margins, AXSM is priced as a high-growth CNS launch stock; proximity to 52wk high (89%) signals market recognition of commercial momentum. Compared to ITCI (~25-30x EV/Sales at comparable stage) and JAZZ (~8x EV/EBITDA post-maturity), AXSM trades rich on current earnings but in-line on a 2-year forward basis if Auvelity ramp sustains.
AXSM.rationale: Axsome is a CNS specialty pharma launch story competing directly with ITCI (lumateperone), ACAD (pimavanserin), JAZZ (sodium oxybate), and SAGE (zuranolone) in treatment-resistant depression and sleep disorders; 93% gross margins and 66% revenue growth match the high-growth CNS launch archetype. Current FCF-negative status is expected mid-launch and does not impair the in-line valuation relative to peers at equivalent commercial stages.

BCRX.relative_comps: BioCryst Pharmaceuticals is a rare disease biotech focused on plasma kallikrein inhibition (berotralstat for hereditary angioedema/HAE) and antiviral nucleosides; true peers are RARE (Ultragenyx, rare metabolic), BMRN (BioMarin, rare enzymes), KalVista (KV1.L, UK-listed plasma kallikrein peer), Ionis (HAE pipeline overlap), and HAE pure-plays Pharming (PHARM.AS) and Takeda's lanadelumab franchise. At p_fcf=6.6x with 98% gross margins and 30% net margin on 94% revenue growth and 3y CAGR of 48%, BCRX appears genuinely cheap vs BMRN (~25x p_fcf, lower growth) and RARE (~20x, similar rare profile). The low multiple likely reflects HAE market size ceiling concerns and competition from subcutaneous prophylactics (Takhzyro/Haegarda), but current earnings power is real and substantially undiscounted vs peers.
BCRX.rationale: BioCryst competes in hereditary angioedema prophylaxis against Takeda (Takhzyro/Haegarda), Pharming (PHARM.AS, Ruconest), and KalVista (KV1.L); broader rare-disease peers are BMRN and RARE. Its p_fcf of 6.6x with 98% gross margin and 30% net margin on near-100% revenue growth is a stark discount to BMRN/RARE at 20-25x FCF on lower growth — the cheap valuation appears to reflect HAE market ceiling fears that are partially overweighted given berotralstat's growing penetration.

CORT.relative_comps: Corcept Therapeutics is a specialty pharma focused on cortisol modulation; its sole commercial product is mifepristone (Korlym) for Cushing's syndrome hypercortisolism. True peers are limited: Recordati (REC.MI) owns Signifor (pasireotide) for Cushing's, HRA Pharma/Strongbridge Biopharma (absorbed by Xeris, XGP.L) with Recorlev (levoketoconazole), and Novo Nordisk's pipeline. Broader orphan endocrine comps include RARE, BMRN, and Spruce Biosciences (SPRB). At p_fcf=74x with 98% gross margin and 13% net margin, CORT trades at a substantial premium to its tiny orphan comp set, justified in part by near-monopoly pricing power in Cushing's. Near 52wk high (94%) and momentum suggest the market has already priced in the durable franchise. Rich vs the few true peers, but franchise quality warrants a premium.
CORT.rationale: Corcept's only real commercial comparables are Recordati's Signifor and Xeris's Recorlev in Cushing's syndrome — an extremely thin peer set where Corcept holds dominant share. Relative to broader rare/orphan-disease pharma (BMRN, RARE) its p_fcf of 74x at only 13% revenue growth is rich; the near-52wk-high positioning and altman_z of 34 reflect a mature, cash-generative franchise but limited upside re-rating catalyst from current valuation.

HRMY.relative_comps: Harmony Biosciences is a commercial CNS specialty pharma focused on narcolepsy (pitolisant/Wakix); true peers are Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ, Xywav/Xyrem sodium oxybate in narcolepsy), Axsome (AXSM, AXS-12 narcolepsy pipeline), and French originator Bioprojet (private, pitolisant EU). At p_fcf=6.0x with 77% gross margins, 18% net margin, 19% ROIC, and 22% revenue growth, HRMY is compellingly cheap relative to JAZZ (~8-10x EV/EBITDA, lower growth) and AXSM (richer on current earnings). The DCF MoS of 57% and IV15 MoS of 50% corroborate the cheap reading. Pitolisant's mechanism novelty (H3 antagonist vs sodium oxybate) provides formulary differentiation, and the 65% 52wk-range position suggests unloved status despite strong fundamentals.
HRMY.rationale: Harmony's only direct product comp is Jazz's narcolepsy franchise (Xywav/Xyrem); AXSM has a late-stage pitolisant competitor. At p_fcf=6x with 77% gross margins, 18% net margin, and 22% revenue growth, HRMY is meaningfully cheaper than JAZZ on FCF and growth-adjusted metrics — the gap is not justified by product risk given Wakix's established payer coverage and differentiated MOA.

INNV.relative_comps: InnovAge Holding (INNV) operates PACE (Program of All-inclusive Care for the Elderly) centers, a hybrid capitated managed care model for frail elderly populations. True peers are Alignment Healthcare (ALHC), CenterWell/Humana's senior-focused primary care, Landmark Health (acquired by Optum), Oak Street Health (acquired by CVS/Aetna), and ChenMed (private). The nearest public comps are ALHC (value-based primary care for seniors) and Astrana Health (ASTH). At p_fcf=55x with 69% gross margin but -4% net margin and negative ROIC (-1.5%), INNV trades rich relative to ALHC (~3-5x EV/Sales at comparable scale/loss stage). The near 52wk high (99%) and small-cap PACE niche suggest market optimism about regulatory normalization post-CMS audit issues, but the premium is hard to justify on current earnings power.
INNV.rationale: InnovAge operates PACE centers for frail elderly, competing with Alignment Healthcare (ALHC), Oak Street Health (CVS), and ChenMed (private) in value-based senior care; at p_fcf=55x with negative net margins and negative ROIC, it trades at a premium to ALHC and comparable value-based care platforms that have similar or better unit economics. The near-52wk-high positioning despite continued losses makes it rich vs peers on any earnings-based metric.

INSP.relative_comps: Inspire Medical Systems makes the Inspire upper airway stimulation (UAS) implant for obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) — a neuromodulation device. True peers are Medtronic (MDT, Restorative Therapies/neuromod division), LivaNova (LIVN, neuromodulation/VNS), Axonics (AXNX, acquired by BD, sacral neuromodulation), and in sleep apnea specifically ResMed (RMD) and Philips Respironics (PHIA.AS) on the CPAP side. At p_fcf=17x with 85% gross margins, 16% net margin, 14% revenue growth (3y CAGR 31%), and near-52wk-low positioning (6%), INSP looks materially cheap relative to RMD (~25x p_fcf, 3% growth) and LIVN (~15x, lower growth) given its superior growth profile. The DCF MoS of -31% is the only cautionary flag, suggesting near-term FCF vs EV framing; the growth/margin combination justifies premium but the 52wk-low position signals sentiment overhang.
INSP.rationale: Inspire competes in implantable neuromodulation for sleep apnea against Medtronic's broader neuromod portfolio, LivaNova, and will eventually face competition from Nyxoah (NYXH); on CPAP treatment alternative basis it competes with ResMed. At p_fcf=17x with 85% gross margins, 31% 3y rev CAGR, and 52wk-low proximity, INSP is cheap relative to ResMed (~25x, 3% growth) and LivaNova; the depressed sentiment is not justified by deteriorating fundamentals.

MOH.relative_comps: Molina Healthcare is a pure-play Medicaid and ACA marketplace managed care organization (MCO); true peers are Centene (CNC), Elevance Health's Medicaid division, Humana (HUM, Medicare-focused but Medicaid overlap), and UnitedHealth (UNH). At p_fcf=0 (likely negative FCF or measurement issue), 9% gross margin (typical for medical-loss-ratio-driven managed care), 1% net margin, and 12% revenue growth, MOH is in-line with CNC on revenue growth but the margin compression (eps_yoy -56%) signals MLR deterioration. Vs CNC at p_fcf ~8-10x and similar Medicaid exposure, MOH's current valuation (mid-52wk range at 57%) looks in-line but the EPS decline is a significant near-term headwind shared across the Medicaid MCO peer group from redetermination headwinds.
MOH.rationale: Molina is a Medicaid-specialist MCO; true peers are Centene (CNC) and WellCare (CNC subsidiary), with UNH/ELV as larger diversified managed care comps. All Medicaid MCOs face the same redetermination/rate headwinds in 2024-2025, so the 1% net margin and EPS compression are sector-wide, not MOH-specific; relative to CNC at comparable scale and Medicaid mix, MOH appears fairly valued.

SAN.PA.relative_comps: Sanofi (SAN.PA) is a French large-cap integrated pharma with leading positions in immunology (Dupixent/dupilumab), rare diseases (Genzyme), and vaccines (Sanofi Pasteur). True peers are Novartis (NVS), Roche (ROG.SW), AstraZeneca (AZN), and to a lesser extent Novo Nordisk (NVO, immunology overlap) and Bristol-Myers (BMY). At p_fcf=9.4x with 70% gross margin, 18% net margin, 10% ROIC, and essentially flat revenue (−1.5%), SAN.PA trades at a meaningful discount to AZN (~15-20x p_fcf, 18% growth) and NVS (~12-14x), and below NVO (~25x on GLP-1 premium). The discount partially reflects Sanofi's decision to increase R&D spend toward pipeline (lowering near-term EPS) and Dupixent royalty structure overhang; the near-52wk-low position (19%) and EPV MoS of +27% suggest genuine cheap-to-intrinsic-value setup.
SAN.PA.rationale: Sanofi competes directly with Novartis, AstraZeneca, and Roche in diversified large pharma and with Regeneron (its Dupixent co-developer) in biologics; at p_fcf=9.4x vs AZN 15-20x and NVS 12-14x, SAN.PA is cheap on FCF multiples for a business with Dupixent's durable growth and a deep vaccine/rare-disease franchise. The near-52wk-low and elevated R&D investment mode create a temporary earnings trough that overstates the structural discount vs peers.

TDOC.relative_comps: Teladoc Health is the largest US telehealth platform (virtual primary care, mental health, chronic condition management); true peers are Amwell (AMWL), Hims & Hers (HIMS), Doximity (DOCS, physician comms/telehealth adjacent), and Accolade (ACCD). At p_fcf=5.2x with 70% gross margins, -8% net margin, -73% ROIC, altman_z=-6.6 (deep distress), and flat-to-declining revenue (−1.5%), TDOC appears statistically cheap on FCF but the altman_z and negative ROIC signal structural impairment from the 2022 Livongo goodwill write-down and persistent inability to convert gross margin to positive earnings. HIMS trades at ~5-8x EV/Sales on positive FCF with +25% growth — a far better business at a similar size. TDOC's DCF MoS of 53% may be a value trap driven by negative ROIC and declining revenue.
TDOC.rationale: Teladoc's true comps are Amwell (AMWL) and Hims & Hers (HIMS) in direct-to-consumer telehealth, and Accolade (ACCD)/Brightspring in employer-sponsored health navigation; the low p_fcf=5.2x is misleading given altman_z=-6.6 and structural ROIC destruction from the Livongo acquisition; vs HIMS (positive FCF, faster growth, stronger unit economics) TDOC is in-line to slightly rich on a quality-adjusted basis despite the nominally cheap multiple.

THC.relative_comps: Tenet Healthcare operates acute-care hospitals and ambulatory surgery centers (Uspi); true peers are HCA Healthcare (HCA, largest US hospital operator), Community Health Systems (CYH), LifePoint Health (private), Surgery Partners (SGRY), and Surgical Care Affiliates. UHS is in this same universe. At p_fcf=6.8x with 82% gross margin (facility-level, pre-SG&A), 7% net margin, 11% ROIC, and flat+3% revenue growth, THC is in-line to cheap vs HCA (~10-12x p_fcf, similar margins, slightly faster growth) and meaningfully cheaper than SGRY (~15x, higher growth ASC mix). The convergence+apex methodology flags suggest multi-method value support. EPS compression (−53%) is a concern but mirrors Tenet's hospital segment margin pressure, while USPI ASC growth is the real value driver.
THC.rationale: Tenet competes directly with HCA (the dominant US for-profit hospital operator), Community Health Systems (CYH), and Surgery Partners (SGRY) in acute hospital and ambulatory surgery; at p_fcf=6.8x vs HCA's 10-12x with comparable margins and USPI's higher-quality ASC earnings stream, THC is cheap vs peers — the discount partially reflects higher leverage and hospital-segment headwinds, but the ASC mix underpin is not fully priced.

UHS.relative_comps: Universal Health Services operates acute-care hospitals and the largest US behavioral health (psychiatric/substance abuse) hospital network; true peers on the behavioral health side are Acadia Healthcare (ACHC) and BrightSpring/Behavioral Health Group (private), and on the acute-care side HCA and THC. At p_fcf=11x with 90% gross margin (facility), 9% net margin, 9% ROIC, 10% revenue growth, and EPS up 37%, UHS is in-line vs THC (p_fcf=6.8x, lower growth) but cheap vs ACHC (~15-18x p_fcf, behavioral health premium). The near-52wk-low position (5%) and multiple apex methodologies (graham_revised MoS 85%, iv15 MoS 36%) suggest a genuine value setup. The behavioral health segment commands a structural premium vs general acute care that is not fully reflected in the current multiple.
UHS.rationale: UHS's behavioral health hospital network (the largest in the US) is the key differentiator from pure acute-care comps like HCA/THC; the closest comparable for that segment is Acadia Healthcare (ACHC) which trades at 15-18x p_fcf. UHS at 11x with 37% EPS growth and 10% revenue growth is cheap both vs ACHC (behavioral premium) and vs HCA/THC on growth-adjusted earnings; near-52wk-low sentiment is at odds with fundamental momentum.

APA.relative_comps: APA trades at p_fcf ~6.7x vs mid-cap E&P peers DVN (~7x), MRO (~8x), CTRA (~7x), and MTDR (~6x), placing it at the cheap-to-in-line end of the peer range. Its ROIC of 20% and net margin of 16% are solid but not exceptional versus Devon or Coterra; the discount largely reflects its Suriname deepwater overhang (capital-intensive, long-dated) and falling revenue (-8% YoY). Price sits 54% of the 52-week range and above the SMA200, suggesting stabilization. Valuation gap vs peers is partly justified by higher geopolitical/execution risk in Suriname; core Permian assets screen cheap on a strip-adjusted basis.
APA.rationale: APA is a US-listed independent E&P with US Permian + North Sea + Egypt + Suriname assets; true peers are US mid-cap E&Ps DVN, MRO, CTRA, OVV, HES and smaller names MTDR and PR. International E&P majors (COP as large-cap anchor) complete the comp set.

ARLP.relative_comps: ARLP (Alliance Resource Partners MLP) trades at p_fcf ~8.1x, a premium to BTU's 5.4x, but ARLP's MLP structure with a ~12% distribution yield and lower financial leverage (Altman-Z 2.9 vs BTU's 1.9) justifies a modest multiple premium. Revenue declining -10% YoY and 3-year CAGR of -3% reflects secular thermal coal demand headwinds, shared by all peers. ARLP's net margin of 14% and ROIC of 18% are superior to BTU (negative net margin), making ARLP the quality name within a structurally declining sector; valuation looks in-line when adjusted for MLP tax efficiency and distribution coverage.
ARLP.rationale: ARLP is a US thermal coal MLP (Illinois Basin/Appalachia); closest peers are Peabody Energy (BTU, also in universe), Arch Resources (ARCH, metallurgical coal), CONSOL Energy (CEIX), and Natural Resource Partners (NRP). The MLP structure distinguishes it from C-corp peers.

BTU.relative_comps: BTU trades at p_fcf ~5.4x, the cheapest in the universe on this metric, but the near-zero gross margin (2.7%) and negative net margin (-1.4%) indicate the current strip price is pressuring unit economics hard. Versus ARLP (also in universe, p_fcf 8.1x, 14% net margin) and ARCH (thermal+met diversified, typically 5-8x), BTU's discount is warranted by its weaker current profitability and more leveraged balance sheet (Altman-Z 1.9). The DCF and owner-earnings MoS scores (~0.52-0.63) suggest intrinsic value well above current price under mid-cycle price assumptions, but near-term fundamentals are pressured; conviction requires a coal price thesis.
BTU.rationale: Peabody Energy is the largest US publicly-traded coal producer (thermal and seaborne met coal); real peers are ARLP (in universe), Arch Resources (ARCH, premium-quality met), CONSOL/CEIX, and international seaborne names like Whitehaven Coal (WHC.ASX) and Yancoal (YAL.ASX) for the export book.

FSLR.relative_comps: FSLR trades at p_fcf ~21.6x and is the only US-domiciled thin-film solar module manufacturer, giving it a structural moat on IRA domestic content adders that Chinese peers (CSIQ, JKS) cannot capture. Its 40.6% gross margin and 29.3% net margin are decisively superior to crystalline-silicon peers (Canadian Solar ~12% gross, JinkoSolar ~13% gross) and module assemblers; the premium multiple is substantially justified by quality. Relative to SHLS (also in universe, solar BOS, 35% gross margin, p_fcf N/A, smaller), FSLR is richer but operates a different part of the value chain. At 49% of the 52-week range and above SMA200, momentum is neutral; IRA policy risk is the key overhang compressing what would otherwise be a richer multiple.
FSLR.rationale: First Solar is a CdTe thin-film solar module manufacturer; true peers are US solar installers/integrators (ENPH, SEDG for inverters/microinverters), downstream integrators (RUN), and Chinese crystalline-silicon manufacturers (CSIQ, JKS). SHLS (in universe) is a BOS/wiring supplier to utility-scale solar — adjacent value-chain, not a direct module competitor.

MAU.PA.relative_comps: MAU.PA (Maurel & Prom) screens with p_fcf reported as 0/NA (likely sparse FCF data on a Paris-listed small-cap) but shows exceptionally high net margin (70.9%) driven by royalty/non-operated income streams from Gabon and Tanzania assets, and a graham_revised MoS of 0.86 suggesting significant book-value support. Revenue fell -25.6% YoY but 3-year CAGR is only -4%, indicating the recent drop is production/asset-related rather than structural. Versus UK-listed African E&P peers Tullow Oil (TLW.L, heavily indebted, negative margins) and Seplat (SEPLAT.L, Nigeria-focused), Maurel screens as higher quality and financially sounder; versus US small-cap E&Ps with similar production scale (BRY, VAALCO), it screens cheap on book and earnings yield. The Paris listing creates a valuation discount relative to US-listed peers.
MAU.PA.rationale: Maurel & Prom (Etablissements Maurel et Prom, MAU.PA) is a French-listed independent E&P with production assets in Gabon, Tanzania, Angola and Colombia; true peers are other Africa-focused independent E&Ps: Tullow Oil (TLW.L), Seplat Energy (SEPLAT.L), Panoro Energy (PANR.L), Vaalco Energy (EGY/VAALCO), and HKN Energy. US small-cap E&Ps (BRY) serve as valuation anchors.

NOG.relative_comps: NOG (Northern Oil & Gas) is a non-operated working interest aggregator in Permian/Williston/Appalachia, trading at p_fcf ~7.5x with only a graham_revised MoS of 0.34 flagging upside — most other valuation methods return N/A, reflecting the asset-light/non-op model's difficulty fitting standard DCF templates. Its gross margin of 32% and ROIC of 17% are respectable for a non-op model, but net margin of just 1.9% (heavy D&D, interest expense) is structurally low. The proximity-to-52wk of only 4% is the most alarming stat: NOG is at a 52-week low, underperforming operated E&P peers like SM (48% of range) and APA (54%). Versus pure non-op peer PHX Minerals (royalty focus) and operated peers SM/CIVI, NOG looks optically cheap but the 52-week price action suggests the market is pricing in leverage/acquisition-pace concerns.
NOG.rationale: Northern Oil & Gas is the largest US non-operated E&P, aggregating working interests across Permian, Williston Basin, and Appalachia; direct peers are other non-op aggregators (PHX Minerals, now royalty-focused), and operated Permian/Williston names (SM, CIVI, MTDR, VTLE) serve as the valuation comp set for the underlying asset base.

OKEA.OL.relative_comps: OKEA ASA (Oslo-listed Norwegian E&P focused on the Norwegian Continental Shelf) shows a revenue YoY collapse of -92.9% and 3-year CAGR of -50%, which almost certainly reflects a significant asset divestiture or production interruption rather than underlying business deterioration — the DCF MoS of 0.93 and EPV MoS of 0.87 would be impossibly high if the revenue drop were structural. Gross margin of 32.9% and net margin of -7% suggest the remaining production base is marginally economic at current Brent. Versus NCS peers Aker BP (AKRBP.OL, large-cap, 35%+ margins, richly valued) and DNO.OL (North Africa/Kurdistan, comparable small-cap), OKEA screens extremely cheap on asset value but the revenue anomaly must be resolved before taking the valuation signal at face value.
OKEA.OL.rationale: OKEA ASA is a Norwegian Continental Shelf E&P listed on Oslo Bors; true peers are other NCS independents: Aker BP (AKRBP.OL), DNO (DNO.OL), Vaar Energi (VAR.OL), and smaller NCS names. The Norwegian listing creates a natural peer cluster distinct from UK North Sea operators (Harbour Energy, EnQuest).

SHLS.relative_comps: Shoals Technologies Group is a solar BOS (balance-of-system) electrical components supplier — wire harnesses, combiners, current-carrying components for utility-scale solar. p_fcf is NA (likely negative FCF or data gap) but revenue growth of +19% YoY and 3-year CAGR of +13% with 35% gross margin and 7% net margin indicates a growth-stage industrial business, not a deep-value play. Versus ENPH (microinverter, 40%+ gross margin, premium multiple) and SEDG (string inverter, under margin pressure), SHLS occupies a more commoditizing niche with less IP differentiation. At 54% of 52-week range, price has recovered from lows. The fundamental_momentum methodology flag and improving EPS (+43% YoY) support the thesis; but margin compression risk from competition is real. Relative to ARRY (tracker manufacturer, comparable utility-scale solar BOS exposure), SHLS screens similarly valued.
SHLS.rationale: Shoals Technologies is a US solar BOS electrical components manufacturer serving utility-scale solar developers; closest competitors are other solar BOS/component suppliers (Array Technologies ARRY for trackers, Nextracker NXT) and inverter makers (ENPH, SEDG). FSLR (in universe) is a module supplier, adjacent but upstream in the value chain.

SM.relative_comps: SM Energy is a Permian Basin (Midland) and South Texas (Eagle Ford) E&P with the strongest ROIC in the universe energy group at 46.7% — substantially above peers CIVI (~20%), MTDR (~25%), and CTRA (~15%) — driven by its low-cost Midland Basin position. p_fcf of 5.3x is the cheapest among the E&P names in the universe and competes with the cheapest mid-cap Permian names in the broader market (VTLE, PR). Revenue grew +18% YoY (vs APA -8%, NOG -3%), reflecting production growth from Permian development. At 48% of 52-week range and above SMA200, SM has underperformed the best Permian names but its ROIC and FCF yield make it a clear relative-value case among operated Permian E&Ps. The valuation gap is not justified by fundamentals — SM screens genuinely cheap.
SM.rationale: SM Energy is a Permian Basin/Eagle Ford E&P; true peers are US-listed mid-cap Permian/multi-basin E&Ps: Civitas Resources (CIVI), Matador Resources (MTDR), Coterra Energy (CTRA), Devon (DVN as large-cap anchor), Permian Resources (PR), and Vaalco/Vital Energy (VTLE). NOG (non-op, in universe) provides a working-interest comparison.

VTOL.relative_comps: Bristow Group (VTOL) is the dominant offshore helicopter services provider to oil majors and national oil companies (North Sea, Gulf of Mexico, West Africa, Australia). p_fcf of 22.4x is elevated and the only positive valuation methodology flagging is graham_revised MoS (0.80), suggesting asset-backed undervaluation despite the earnings multiple looking rich. ROIC of 2.4% is low — reflective of the capital-intensive fleet ownership model — and net margin of 8.7% on 5.3% revenue growth is modest. The true peer set (PHX.OL on Oslo Bors for North Sea helicopter ops, CHC Group private, and decommissioned ERA) is thin; Bristow is effectively a near-monopoly post-industry consolidation. Versus OFS giants SLB and HAL (higher margins, diversified), VTOL is a smaller, more capital-intensive sub-sector. The graham_revised signal (asset-based value) is the most credible; earnings-based multiples look stretched for the ROIC delivered.
VTOL.rationale: Bristow Group is a US-listed offshore helicopter services company (oil & gas crew transportation); it has no direct publicly-traded pure-play peers following industry consolidation — PHX.OL (Helianor, Norway) and the private CHC Group are the closest. OFS service comparables (SLB, HAL, OII for subsea) provide a sector valuation anchor. The near-monopoly position post-merger with Era Aviation means single-company risk is high.

CMCSA.relative_comps: CMCSA trades at p_fcf ~3.9x — a deep-discount to cable/telco peers: Charter (CHTR) ~7x, T ~10x, VZ ~8x — reflecting secular broadband subscriber erosion and cord-cutting pressure that the market has been pricing in for years. Its gross margin of 72% and net margin of 16% are superior to pure telco peers, and EPS grew +30% YoY (largely buyback-driven), so the valuation gap vs. CHTR is not fully justified by quality; CMCSA has better margins and a larger content/theme-park business. It sits near its 52-week low and below SMA200, suggesting persistent selling; cheap vs. peers on pure FCF yield but the narrative headwind (video sub losses) is real. Verdict: genuinely cheap vs. the peer set — the discount exceeds the secular risk differential.
CMCSA.rationale: CMCSA is Comcast — a US cable/broadband/media conglomerate (Xfinity cable, NBCUniversal, Sky). True peers are Charter Communications (CHTR), AT&T (T), Verizon (VZ) on the distribution side and Paramount Global (PARA), Warner Bros Discovery (WBD), Disney (DIS) on the content side, with Netflix (NFLX) as the disruptive OTT benchmark. TMUS adds a wireless/broadband overlap angle.

FNTN.DE.relative_comps: FNTN.DE (Freenet AG, Germany) is a mobile virtual network operator and digital lifestyle/TV streaming reseller — not an infrastructure telco — so its true comparables are MVNO/digital reseller models and European value telecom operators. At p_fcf ~7.1x and proximity_52wk=0.0 (at 52-week low), it screens cheaper than Deutsche Telekom (DTE.DE ~12x FCF) and in-line with distressed infrastructure plays like Vodafone (VOD.L) and Telecom Italia (TIT.MI). Its roic of 10.7% and net margin 11.1% are solid for an asset-light MVNO, materially above TIT.MI or VOD.L on returns. Revenue trend is mildly negative (-1.5% YoY), reflecting German mobile price pressure, but the model is capital-light. Cheap vs. the infrastructure telcos on FCF given the asset-light profile.
FNTN.DE.rationale: FNTN.DE is Freenet AG, a German MVNO and digital services/TV-streaming reseller (trades on XETRA). It does not own mobile network infrastructure — it resells capacity from Telekom/Vodafone/O2. Real peers are other European MVNOs and value-tier telcos: Deutsche Telekom (DTE.DE), Vodafone (VOD.L), Telefonica (TEF.MC), BT Group (BT.A), Orange (ORAN.PA), Telecom Italia (TIT.MI). LUMN (Lumen) adds the distressed/declining-revenue telco angle.

FOX.relative_comps: FOX (non-voting shares) and FOXA (voting shares) are essentially the same economic interest at near-identical FCF multiples (~7x) — FOX's discount to FOXA is purely a governance/voting premium. Vs. the broader media peer set, FOX screens materially cheap: WBD trades ~8-10x FCF with far more leverage, Paramount (PARA) is structurally loss-making, Disney carries an enormous content debt load. FOX's 33% gross margin and 14% net margin are best-in-class among legacy broadcast peers, its balance sheet is clean, and revenue grew +17% YoY driven by political ad spend and sports rights strength. Near 52-week lows with strong EPS growth (+57% YoY) is a classic value set-up; the cheap rating is justified by structural secular concerns (linear TV decline) but the FCF generation and clean balance sheet suggest those concerns are already embedded.
FOX.rationale: FOX Corp (non-voting Class B shares) is a US broadcast/cable news and sports company (Fox News, Fox Sports, Fox Broadcasting). True peers are FOXA (same company, voting shares), Warner Bros Discovery (WBD), Paramount Global (PARA), AMC Networks (AMCX), and News Corp (NEWS — same Murdoch family ecosystem). Netflix (NFLX) and Disney (DIS) are the quality benchmarks that price the secular decline discount in traditional media.

FOXA.relative_comps: FOXA (voting shares) trades at p_fcf ~7.7x vs. FOX's ~7.1x — a modest ~9% voting premium which is normal for dual-class structures. The fundamental analysis is identical to FOX: both reflect the same underlying business with best-in-class margins among legacy broadcast peers (+33% gross, +14% net), clean balance sheet, and strong revenue momentum (+17% YoY, +57% EPS). Near 52-week lows despite fundamental strength indicates continued legacy-media de-rating pressure. The premium over FOX is justified (voting control over a cash-generative media asset), and both are cheap vs. the broader media peer set on FCF yield.
FOXA.rationale: FOXA is Fox Corporation Class A voting shares — economically identical to FOX (Class B non-voting) but conferring shareholder voting rights. The same competitor set applies: WBD, PARA, AMCX, NEWS, with DIS and NFLX as quality anchor comparisons. The FOX/FOXA spread is a pure governance-premium analysis, not a separate business.

MMT.PA.relative_comps: MMT.PA is M6 Group (Metropole Television), a French free-to-air and pay-TV broadcaster. At p_fcf ~11x it is modestly more expensive than TFI.PA (TF1, p_fcf ~13x — same sector, French FTA) on this metric but TF1 has higher multi-method MoS scores. Both face revenue headwinds from linear TV audience fragmentation (-4.3% revenue YoY for MMT.PA, -2.5% for TFI.PA). M6's ROIC of 13% and net margin of ~9.8% are comparable to TF1's 9.4% / 6.7% — M6 is operationally more efficient. European commercial broadcaster peers (ITV.L, RTL, Mediaset MEDI.MI) all trade in the 7-12x FCF range; MMT.PA at 11x sits mid-range. Near its 52-week mid-range (proximity 0.35), it is in-line to mildly cheap vs. the true broadcaster peer set given its superior ROIC.
MMT.PA.rationale: MMT.PA is M6 Group / Metropole Television SA, a French commercial broadcaster (RTL Group was a major shareholder until 2022 failed merger with TF1). True peers are TFI.PA (TF1 Group, in-universe), RTL Group (German/European broadcaster), ITV.L (UK commercial broadcaster), Mediaset Italia (MEDI.MI), and Antenna TV (TEN.AT). French mid-cap M6 is a pure-play FTA commercial TV operator competing primarily with TF1 domestically.

OMC.relative_comps: OMC (Omnicom Group) is executing a transformative merger with Interpublic (IPG) to create the world's largest ad holding group. At p_fcf ~5.4x and with +10% revenue growth, Omnicom screens cheaper than Publicis (PUB.PA, in-universe, p_fcf ~7.5x) — a meaningful valuation gap given that Omnicom's scale, ROIC (10.6%), and revenue growth are comparable. WPP.L trades at ~6-7x FCF with worse growth; IPG (merger target) is at ~7x. The negative reported net margin (-0.3%) likely reflects merger/restructuring charges and is transient; underlying FCF generation is strong (p_fcf 5.4x). PUB.PA is the most direct comparable and trades at a 40% premium — arguably unjustified given Omnicom's stronger top-line momentum. Cheap vs. the peer set.
OMC.rationale: OMC is Omnicom Group, a US advertising/marketing holding company (BBDO, DDB, TBWA). True peers are Publicis Groupe (PUB.PA — in-universe), Interpublic Group (IPG — pending merger target), WPP plc (WPP.L), Havas (HAVAS.PA after spin-off from Vivendi), and smaller digital-first groups like Dentsu (DEN.DE) and S4 Capital (S4C.L).

PRX.AS.relative_comps: PRX.AS is Prosus NV, the Amsterdam-listed consumer internet holding company spun out of Naspers (NPN.JO) — it holds a ~26% stake in Tencent (TCEHY) plus e-commerce/food-delivery assets (iFood, OLX, Swiggy). The p_fcf of 49.7x is almost entirely notional: most of Prosus's value derives from the Tencent stake, and the 'net margin of 200%' is from periodic stake sales, not operating income. The true comparable is the NAV discount — Prosus historically trades at a 40-50% discount to its underlying Tencent stake value, and the extreme proximity_52wk of 0.02 (near 52-week low) reflects continued investor frustration with this discount compression thesis. On a sum-of-parts basis, PRX.AS remains materially cheap vs. its intrinsic asset value; as a standalone operating business it is not profitable (ROIC -1.2%). The gap is partially justified by holding company discount, capital allocation uncertainty, and South African parent overhang.
PRX.AS.rationale: PRX.AS is Prosus NV (Amsterdam), the consumer internet investment arm of South Africa's Naspers (NPN.JO). It is primarily a Tencent (TCEHY) holding vehicle (~26% stake, worth more than Prosus's entire market cap), with secondary holdings in MercadoLibre-equivalent (iFood Brazil), OLX classifieds, Swiggy (India food delivery), and EdTech. The peer frame is NAV-discount holding companies: Naspers parent (NPN.JO), and the underlying assets benchmark against Sea Ltd (SE), MercadoLibre (MELI), Grab (GRAB). Bollore (BN.PA) is a European holding-company-at-discount analog.

PUB.PA.relative_comps: PUB.PA (Publicis Groupe) is the premium-rated advertising holding company in this universe, trading at p_fcf ~7.5x vs. OMC (in-universe) at ~5.4x — a ~40% premium. Publicis commands this premium due to its stronger positioning in data/technology-led marketing (Epsilon, Sapient, Publicis.Sapient), faster organic growth, and a near-52-week-high stance (proximity 0.65). Revenue grew +8.5% YoY vs. WPP.L declining and IPG flat. Its 9.4% ROIC and net margin of 9.5% (on net revenue basis, so true gross is 100%) are consistent with industry leaders. The premium is partially justified by the data tech stack differentiation vs. Omnicom's more traditional creative model, but the ~40% gap to OMC on FCF feels stretched if the Omnicom-IPG merger closes — that combined entity would match or exceed Publicis in scale. In-line to mildly rich relative to peers.
PUB.PA.rationale: PUB.PA is Publicis Groupe (Paris), a top-3 global advertising/marketing holding company (Leo Burnett, Saatchi, Epsilon data platform). Direct competitors are Omnicom (OMC — in-universe), Interpublic (IPG), WPP plc (WPP.L), Havas (HAVAS.PA), and Dentsu (DEN.DE). S4 Capital (S4C.L) represents the disruptive digital-native agency model.

TFI.PA.relative_comps: TFI.PA (TF1 Group) trades at p_fcf ~13.2x, modestly higher than in-universe peer MMT.PA (M6, ~11x), reflecting TF1's larger scale and position as France's leading commercial broadcaster. However, TF1 has weaker margins than M6 (net margin 6.7% vs. M6's 9.8%, ROIC 9.4% vs. 13%) and declining revenue (-2.5% YoY) and EPS (-26% YoY), making the premium to M6 hard to justify on fundamentals alone — it likely reflects TF1's brand/audience leadership premium. At proximity_52wk=0.028 (near 52-week low), TF1 is deeply out of favor. Vs. ITV.L (~7-8x FCF) and Mediaset (~8x), TFI.PA is mildly rich; vs. US broadcast peers like FOX at ~7x, TFI.PA is notably expensive for a smaller, declining-revenue business. Rich relative to European broadcast peers on a pure FCF basis.
TFI.PA.rationale: TFI.PA is TF1 Group, France's largest free-to-air commercial TV broadcaster (TF1, TMC, TFX channels plus streaming via TF1+). True peers are MMT.PA (M6 Group — in-universe, direct French FTA competitor), RTL Group (pan-European commercial TV), ITV.L (UK commercial broadcaster), Mediaset Italia (MEDI.MI), and Antenna TV Greece (TEN.AT). The French FTA duopoly context (TF1 vs. M6) makes MMT.PA the single most important comparable.

YELP.relative_comps: YELP is a US local business reviews and advertising marketplace. At p_fcf ~4.8x with 90% gross margins, 9.9% net margin, and a DCF MoS of 0.65, it screens as materially cheap vs. any comparable internet marketplace. TripAdvisor (TRIP) trades at ~8-12x FCF with inferior margins and negative growth; Angi (ANGI) is structurally unprofitable; Google Maps/Search (GOOGL) — the existential threat — trades at 25x+ FCF. The cheap valuation reflects genuine secular risk: Google's continuous local search improvements eat directly into Yelp's advertising wallet share, making this a value trap risk. Revenue growth has decelerated to +3.7% YoY and the stock sits in the bottom quartile of its 52-week range (proximity 0.27). The FCF yield is exceptional for an asset-light marketplace, but the moat erosion from Google is real and the market prices this correctly as a discount; cheap but with justified structural discount.
YELP.rationale: YELP is a US consumer review and local-business advertising platform. Closest business-model peers are TripAdvisor (TRIP — reviews/local marketplace), Angi (ANGI — home services marketplace), and Thumbtack. Google Maps/Local (GOOGL) and Apple Maps are the dominant structural competitors. The Trade Desk (TTD) and ZoomInfo (ZI) are adjacent SMB advertising and local-data comparables. Yelp's economics (high gross margin, pure software ad model) resemble a scaled local SaaS business more than a media company.

ACA.PA.relative_comps: Crédit Agricole trades at p_fcf of 2.7x — meaningfully cheaper than BNP Paribas (~5-6x) and peer Société Générale (~4x) — a discount justified in part by its cooperative ownership structure and lower trading-book leverage, but also partly a genuine mispricing given 3-year revenue CAGR of 35% and improving NII. At a proximity of 0.58 to its 52-week high with EPV MoS of 0.62 and Graham MoS of 0.77, it screens cheap vs the European universal-bank peer set on both valuation and momentum; net margin of 6.3% is in line with continental peers. The gap to BNP is unjustified by quality differentials alone — ACA's retail franchise, CIB scale and Amundi asset-management arm give it comparable earnings quality.
ACA.PA.rationale: ACA.PA is Crédit Agricole SA, a top-3 French universal bank (Euronext Paris). True comps are the other large European universal/diversified banks: BNP Paribas (BNP.PA), Société Générale (GLE.PA), Deutsche Bank (DBK.DE), ING Groep (INGA.AS), CaixaBank (CABK.MC), Barclays (BARC.L), UniCredit (UCG.MI).

BMPS.MI.relative_comps: Monte dei Paschi trades at p_fcf of 10.9x — rich relative to Italian peers UniCredit (~6x) and Intesa Sanpaolo (~7x) — and its 3-year revenue CAGR of 6.6% plus flat EPS disguise a -28% revenue YoY print, suggesting the elevated multiple reflects a earnings-recovery narrative (net margin of 64% is artificially high, a function of reserve releases and the 2022-2024 government recapitalisation, not durable operating profitability). Proximity to 52-week high is 0.95 meaning the stock is near peaks yet EPV MoS is near zero (0.02) and DCF MoS is slightly negative — the valuation gap to better-capitalised Italian peers is not justified by quality. Altman-Z of -0.44 signals ongoing balance-sheet fragility.
BMPS.MI.rationale: BMPS.MI is Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena, Italy's third-largest bank by assets and the world's oldest operating bank, listed on Borsa Italiana. True comps are Italian and Southern European regional/universal banks: UniCredit (UCG.MI), Intesa Sanpaolo (ISP.MI), BPER Banca (BPER.MI), Banco BPM (BPE.MI), and cross-border comparables Crédit Agricole (ACA.PA), Société Générale (GLE.PA), BBVA (BBVA.MC).

HNR1.DE.relative_comps: Hannover Re trades at p_fcf of 4.7x — the cheapest of the three reinsurers in this universe (vs SREN.SW at 12.1x and SCR.PA at 5.2x) — with a DCF MoS of 0.69 and iv15 MoS of 0.88, making it the strongest valuation case in the group. Its proximity to the 52-week high is only 0.26, suggesting price weakness despite strong fundamentals: eps_yoy of +13.4% and net margin of 9.85% compare well to Swiss Re's 9.9% and comfortably beat SCOR's 5.75%. Versus Munich Re (MUV2.DE), Hannover Re typically trades at a modest P/E discount (~10-15%), which is appropriate given Munich Re's primary-insurance diversification, but the current gap on FCF basis appears wider than justified by the quality differential.
HNR1.DE.rationale: HNR1.DE is Hannover Rück SE (Hannover Re), the world's third-largest reinsurer by premium volume, listed on XETRA. True comps are the global reinsurance oligopoly: Munich Re (MUV2.DE), Swiss Re (SREN.SW), SCOR (SCR.PA), Reinsurance Group of America (RGA), Everest Group (EG), and Covéa/partnered specialist (CS.PA is AXA in practice — note: the actual fourth global reinsurer is General Re/Berkshire, not exchange-listed separately; PartnerRe was privatised by Covéa).

LNC.relative_comps: Lincoln National trades with zero computable p_fcf (negative or near-zero FCF in the trailing period) but EPV MoS of 0.87 and DCF MoS of 0.61 suggest deeply discounted normalised earnings power vs intrinsic value — however eps_yoy of -68% and roic_avg of 0.57% flag that current earnings are severely depressed, most likely reflecting reserve strengthening charges and the 2022-2023 long-term care / variable annuity reserve shock. Versus MetLife (MET) and Prudential (PRU) — both trading at 8-10x earnings with mid-single-digit ROE — Lincoln screens optically cheap but the quality gap is real: MET and PRU have more diversified liability profiles and lower sensitivity to interest-rate reserve marks. The discount appears partly earned and partly excessive if management executes on the recapitalisation plan.
LNC.rationale: LNC is Lincoln National Corporation, a US life insurance and annuities writer (NYSE). True comps are US life/annuity and group benefit insurers: MetLife (MET), Prudential Financial (PRU), Principal Financial Group (PFG), Unum Group (UNM), CNO Financial (CNO), and international peer Aflac (AFL). The company is NOT a property-casualty insurer; its core businesses are life insurance, annuities, group benefits and retirement plan services.

SCR.PA.relative_comps: SCOR trades at p_fcf of 5.2x — in between Hannover Re (4.7x, cheaper) and Swiss Re (12.1x, richer) — with a very strong DCF MoS of 0.84 and EPV MoS of 0.80, the best valuation readings in the reinsurance trio here. Its proximity to the 52-week high is 0.82 (price strength), yet net margin of 5.75% is the weakest of the three, and revenue_yoy is slightly negative (-5.4%); the enormous eps_yoy number reflects a normalisation from a near-zero base after the 2022 nat-cat loss year and Vestergaard CEO transition disruption. Versus Hannover Re, SCOR appears slightly rich on FCF but comparably cheap on normalised intrinsic value; the gap is partly justified by SCOR's smaller scale and higher life-reinsurance concentration (lower predictability than P&C-heavy HNR1).
SCR.PA.rationale: SCR.PA is SCOR SE, a French global reinsurer listed on Euronext Paris, the fifth-largest reinsurer globally. True comps are the global reinsurance peer set: Munich Re (MUV2.DE), Swiss Re (SREN.SW), Hannover Re (HNR1.DE), Reinsurance Group of America (RGA), Everest Group (EG), and QBE Insurance (QBE.AX) for a non-European angle.

SEZL.relative_comps: Sezzle trades at p_fcf of 29.3x, which looks rich in absolute terms but is the correct lens for a high-growth BNPL/credit-services company with revenue CAGR of 53% over 3 years, gross margin of 85% and net margin of nearly 30% — metrics that are substantially above Affirm (AFRM, gross margin ~55%, net margin deeply negative) and LendingClub (LC, net margin ~10%). Among US BNPL/fintech credit peers, Sezzle's unit economics and profitability profile are genuinely differentiated; the p_fcf of 29x vs a 66% revenue growth rate implies a growth-adjusted PEG well below 1x, comparable to where Upstart (UPST) traded when it was reliably profitable. Proximity to 52-week high of 0.88 reflects positive momentum; the main risk premium is small-cap liquidity and credit-cycle exposure.
SEZL.rationale: SEZL is Sezzle Inc, a US-listed (Nasdaq) buy-now-pay-later and consumer credit services company targeting younger, underbanked consumers. True comps are BNPL and consumer fintech credit platforms: Affirm Holdings (AFRM), Upstart Holdings (UPST), Block/Square's BNPL arm (SQ), SoFi Technologies (SOFI), LendingClub (LC), and internationally Zip Co (ZIP.AX) and Laybuy (now delisted). PayPal (PYPL) is a relevant adjacent competitor via its BNPL feature.

SREN.SW.relative_comps: Swiss Re trades at p_fcf of 12.1x — materially richer than Hannover Re (4.7x) and SCOR (5.2x) — despite comparable or slightly better net margins (9.9% vs 9.85% for Hannover, 5.75% for SCOR) and a lower proximity to 52-week high (0.32). The DCF MoS of 0.16 and EPV MoS of -0.26 confirm the market is pricing Swiss Re at or above fair value on normalised earnings power; the Graham MoS of 0.32 provides only modest book-value support. Swiss Re's premium over the reinsurance peer set reflects its scale (second-largest globally), balance-sheet quality, sigma advisory franchise and L&H reinsurance diversification, but the gap to Hannover Re specifically appears excessive given Hannover's superior FCF yield and iv15 MoS of 0.88. The stock screens in-line with Munich Re but rich vs the broader peer group.
SREN.SW.rationale: SREN.SW is Swiss Re Ltd, the world's second-largest reinsurer by premium volume, listed on the SIX Swiss Exchange. True comps are the global reinsurance oligopoly: Munich Re (MUV2.DE), Hannover Re (HNR1.DE), SCOR (SCR.PA), Reinsurance Group of America (RGA), Everest Group (EG), and AXA XL/AXA SA (AXAHY) as a primary+reinsurance conglomerate comparator.
